6.2.1 Interpreting the listing QA diagnostics
Missingness: missing prices or areas are not zero values — they are unobserved. Zone medians will be computed from observed listings only. High missingness for a key variable (>20% of price_eur) should trigger a decision about whether to use that variable as the primary outcome or demote it to a robustness check.
Coordinate quality and zone_match_method: listings assigned by nearest-zone proximity are not automatically wrong, but they inflate the apparent supply in peripheral zones and can create spatial autocorrelation artefacts that mimic genuine neighbourhood price spillovers. The fallback_assignment_share column in master_zones will flag zones where this matters.
Condition score decision: infer_condition_score maps preservation/condition labels to an ordinal score only when the observed values form a defensible rank order. If the decision shows condition_score_created: False, the model will use category share columns as proxies instead.
crumbs: 2  ETE TOPIC 3 :: Explanatory Spatial Econometric Models

title: 2  # ETE TOPIC 3 :: Explanatory Spatial Econometric Models
section: 6.3 A3. Zones, Parishes, and Analytical Supports
text:
6.3 A3. Zones, Parishes, and Analytical Supports
The same listing points can be aggregated to zones, parishes, square grids, or census tracts. The spatial pattern of prices and the Moran statistic will differ across these choices. This is the Modifiable Areal Unit Problem (MAUP): the apparent spatial structure of the housing market depends partly on the analytical support chosen, not just on the underlying market process.
What the code does: - Joins listing-level data to zone polygons via zone_id, computing zone-level medians, means, IQRs, category shares, fallback-assignment rates, and outlier shares via aggregate_listing_support. - Creates master_zones as the primary modelling table (all 131 zones, including those with no listings). - If the parish layer is available, creates master_parishes as a parallel sensitivity support. - Reports a support summary: how many zones have at least one listing, fewer than 3, 5, or 10.


▶ Show code

if ZONE_ID not in listings_3763.columns or ZONE_ID not in zones_3763.columns:
    raise ValueError(f"Expected join key {ZONE_ID!r} is required in listings and zones.")

zone_aggregates = aggregate_listing_support(listings_3763, ZONE_ID)
master_zones = zones_3763.merge(zone_aggregates, on=ZONE_ID, how="left")
master_zones = fill_support_count_flags(master_zones)

zone_support_summary = pd.DataFrame(
    [
        {
            "support": "zones",
            "n_supports": len(master_zones),
            "supports_with_listings": int((master_zones["n_listings"] > 0).sum()),
            "supports_without_listings": int((master_zones["n_listings"] == 0).sum()),
            "supports_n_lt3": int(master_zones["small_n_lt3"].sum()),
            "supports_n_lt5": int(master_zones["small_n_lt5"].sum()),
            "supports_n_lt10": int(master_zones["small_n_lt10"].sum()),
            "total_listings_aggregated": int(master_zones["n_listings"].sum()),
        }
    ]
)
display_table(zone_support_summary)

display_cols = existing_columns(
    master_zones,
    [
        ZONE_ID,
        "zone_name",
        "municipality_name",
        "n_listings",
        "median_price_eur",
        "mean_price_eur",
        "median_unit_price_eur_m2",
        "mean_unit_price_eur_m2",
        "median_area_living_m2",
        "mean_log_price_eur",
        "median_log_price_eur",
        "mean_log_unit_price_eur_m2",
        "median_log_unit_price_eur_m2",
        "mean_log_area_living_m2",
        "mean_condition_score",
        "median_condition_score",
        "fallback_assignment_share",
        "small_n_lt3",
        "small_n_lt5",
        "small_n_lt10",
    ],
)
show_object(master_zones[display_cols].sort_values("n_listings", ascending=False).head(12))

master_parishes = None
parish_support_summary = None

if freguesias_3763 is not None:
    parish_id_col = "DTMNFR21" if "DTMNFR21" in freguesias_3763.columns else "freguesia"
    parish_name_col = "freguesia" if "freguesia" in freguesias_3763.columns else parish_id_col
    parish_joined = spatial_join_with_fallback(
        listings_3763,
        freguesias_3763,
        columns=[parish_id_col, parish_name_col] if parish_id_col != parish_name_col else [parish_id_col],
        predicate="within",
    )
    if parish_joined[parish_id_col].isna().any():
        parish_joined_intersects = spatial_join_with_fallback(
            listings_3763,
            freguesias_3763,
            columns=[parish_id_col, parish_name_col] if parish_id_col != parish_name_col else [parish_id_col],
            predicate="intersects",
        )
        parish_joined[parish_id_col] = parish_joined[parish_id_col].fillna(parish_joined_intersects[parish_id_col])
        if parish_name_col in parish_joined.columns and parish_name_col in parish_joined_intersects.columns:
            parish_joined[parish_name_col] = parish_joined[parish_name_col].fillna(
                parish_joined_intersects[parish_name_col]
            )

    parish_aggregates = aggregate_listing_support(parish_joined, parish_id_col)
    master_parishes = freguesias_3763.merge(parish_aggregates, on=parish_id_col, how="left")
    master_parishes = fill_support_count_flags(master_parishes)
    parish_support_summary = pd.DataFrame(
        [
            {
                "support": "parishes/freguesias",
                "n_supports": len(master_parishes),
                "supports_with_listings": int((master_parishes["n_listings"] > 0).sum()),
                "supports_without_listings": int((master_parishes["n_listings"] == 0).sum()),
                "supports_n_lt3": int(master_parishes["small_n_lt3"].sum()),
                "supports_n_lt5": int(master_parishes["small_n_lt5"].sum()),
                "supports_n_lt10": int(master_parishes["small_n_lt10"].sum()),
                "total_listings_aggregated": int(master_parishes["n_listings"].sum()),
            }
        ]
    )
    display_table(parish_support_summary)
    parish_display_cols = existing_columns(
        master_parishes,
        [
            parish_id_col,
            parish_name_col,
            "n_listings",
            "median_price_eur",
            "median_unit_price_eur_m2",
            "median_area_living_m2",
            "mean_condition_score",
            "fallback_assignment_share",
            "small_n_lt5",
            "small_n_lt10",
        ],
    )
    show_object(master_parishes[parish_display_cols].sort_values("n_listings", ascending=False))
else:
    print("No parish/freguesia layer detected; master_parishes was not created.")

6.3.1 Interpreting the zone support summary
Zones with zero listings are a normal feature of a well-specified modelling table, not an error. They arise when a zone covers an area where no dwellings were listed during 2018–2022. Zero-listing zones should not be dropped silently: in spatial modelling they affect contiguity relationships and Moran statistics. A zone that is a spatial neighbour of high-price zones but has no observations is a missing value in the Moran calculation — a materially different signal from a low-price zone.
Small-N flags (small_n_lt3, small_n_lt5, small_n_lt10) record zones where the median price estimate is based on very few listings. A zone-level median from 2 listings has a wide implicit confidence interval and is strongly influenced by the composition of those 2 listings. The robustness matrix (section A12) will re-run models after filtering to zones with ≥3 and ≥5 listings.
Fallback assignment share: zones with >25% nearest-zone fallback assignments should be interpreted cautiously; their price aggregates may include listings more accurately attributed to adjacent zones.

title: 2  # ETE TOPIC 3 :: Explanatory Spatial Econometric Models
section: 6.4 A4. Exploratory Spatial Analysis: Maps and Ranked Diagnostics
text:
6.4 A4. Exploratory Spatial Analysis: Maps and Ranked Diagnostics
Before any model, inspect the raw spatial distribution of prices. Exploratory maps serve two purposes: (1) motivating the need for spatial modelling by showing prices are not randomly distributed in space, and (2) revealing data-quality or composition anomalies that would distort model estimates.
What the code does: Produces three choropleth maps of master_zones (median total asking price, median unit price, median living area) and a ranked diagnostic table of the highest-price zones (among those with ≥3 listings).


▶ Show code

display_table(numeric_summary(listings_3763, [TARGET_PRICE, TARGET_UNIT_PRICE, AREA_VAR]))

for col in existing_columns(
    listings_3763,
    ["municipality_name", "property_type_std", "typology_bucket_std", "condition_std", "preservation_class_std", "listing_year"],
):
    display_table(value_counts_table(listings_3763, col))

local_support_diagnostics = pd.DataFrame(
    [
        {
            "support": "zones",
            "n_supports": len(master_zones),
            "supports_with_no_listings": int((master_zones["n_listings"] == 0).sum()),
            "supports_n_lt3": int(master_zones["small_n_lt3"].sum()),
            "supports_n_lt5": int(master_zones["small_n_lt5"].sum()),
            "supports_n_lt10": int(master_zones["small_n_lt10"].sum()),
            "median_n_listings": float(master_zones["n_listings"].median()),
            "max_n_listings": int(master_zones["n_listings"].max()),
        }
    ]
)
if master_parishes is not None:
    local_support_diagnostics = pd.concat(
        [
            local_support_diagnostics,
            pd.DataFrame(
                [
                    {
                        "support": "parishes/freguesias",
                        "n_supports": len(master_parishes),
                        "supports_with_no_listings": int((master_parishes["n_listings"] == 0).sum()),
                        "supports_n_lt3": int(master_parishes["small_n_lt3"].sum()),
                        "supports_n_lt5": int(master_parishes["small_n_lt5"].sum()),
                        "supports_n_lt10": int(master_parishes["small_n_lt10"].sum()),
                        "median_n_listings": float(master_parishes["n_listings"].median()),
                        "max_n_listings": int(master_parishes["n_listings"].max()),
                    }
                ]
            ),
        ],
        ignore_index=True,
    )
display_table(local_support_diagnostics)

zone_exploratory_cols = existing_columns(
    master_zones,
    [
        ZONE_ID,
        "zone_name",
        "municipality_name",
        "n_listings",
        "median_price_eur",
        "median_unit_price_eur_m2",
        "median_area_living_m2",
        "mean_condition_score",
        "property_type_std_share_house",
        "property_type_std_share_apartment",
        "fallback_assignment_share",
        "unit_price_outlier_share",
        "small_n_lt5",
        "small_n_lt10",
    ],
)
show_object(master_zones[zone_exploratory_cols].sort_values("n_listings", ascending=False).head(15))

plot_point_map(
    listings_3763,
    TARGET_UNIT_PRICE,
    "Aveiro/Ilhavo listings: unit asking price per square metre",
)

plot_layer_map(
    master_zones,
    "median_unit_price_eur_m2",
    "Aveiro/Ilhavo zones: median unit asking price",
)
plot_layer_map(
    master_zones,
    "n_listings",
    "Aveiro/Ilhavo zones: number of observed listings",
    cmap="magma",
)
plot_layer_map(
    master_zones,
    "small_n_lt10",
    "Aveiro/Ilhavo zones: small-N flag (n < 10)",
    cmap="Set2",
)

for diagnostic_col, diagnostic_title in [
    ("mean_condition_score", "Aveiro/Ilhavo zones: mean condition/preservation score"),
    ("property_type_std_share_house", "Aveiro/Ilhavo zones: share of house listings"),
    ("typology_bucket_std_share_t3", "Aveiro/Ilhavo zones: share of T3 listings"),
    ("fallback_assignment_share", "Aveiro/Ilhavo zones: nearest-zone fallback share"),
    ("unit_price_outlier_share", "Aveiro/Ilhavo zones: IQR outlier share in unit prices"),
]:
    if diagnostic_col in master_zones.columns:
        plot_layer_map(master_zones, diagnostic_col, diagnostic_title, cmap="cividis")

if master_parishes is not None:
    plot_layer_map(
        master_parishes,
        "median_unit_price_eur_m2",
        "Aveiro/Ilhavo parishes: median unit asking price",
    )
    plot_layer_map(
        master_parishes,
        "n_listings",
        "Aveiro/Ilhavo parishes: number of observed listings",
        cmap="magma",
    )
    plot_layer_map(
        master_parishes,
        "small_n_lt10",
        "Aveiro/Ilhavo parishes: small-N flag (n < 10)",
        cmap="Set2",
    )
    for diagnostic_col, diagnostic_title in [
        ("mean_condition_score", "Aveiro/Ilhavo parishes: mean condition/preservation score"),
        ("property_type_std_share_house", "Aveiro/Ilhavo parishes: share of house listings"),
        ("fallback_assignment_share", "Aveiro/Ilhavo parishes: nearest-zone fallback share"),
        ("unit_price_outlier_share", "Aveiro/Ilhavo parishes: IQR outlier share in unit prices"),
    ]:
        if diagnostic_col in master_parishes.columns:
            plot_layer_map(master_parishes, diagnostic_col, diagnostic_title, cmap="cividis")

hist_cols = existing_columns(listings_3763, [TARGET_PRICE, TARGET_UNIT_PRICE, AREA_VAR, "log_price_eur", "log_unit_price_eur_m2"])
if hist_cols:
    axes = listings_3763[hist_cols].hist(figsize=(12, 8), bins=30)
    plt.suptitle("Listing-level distributions before spatial modelling")
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

6.4.1 Interpreting the exploratory maps
Median total price vs. median unit price: these two maps tell different stories. A zone with high median total price but moderate unit price most likely contains larger dwellings (houses rather than apartments). A zone with high unit price but moderate total price typically contains smaller, higher-quality, or more centrally located units. Interpreting total price as a quality measure is misleading when dwelling sizes vary systematically across zones.
Spatial clustering: if the maps show visible clusters of high-price and low-price zones — contiguous blocks of similar colours — this is the first visual evidence of positive spatial autocorrelation. Moran’s I (section A7) will quantify and test this pattern. Recall that Moran’s I and W were introduced in the ESDA sessions (Sessions 1–2); here they bridge from description to model choice.
Size map: comparing the size map with the unit-price map helps distinguish genuinely high-value locations from artefacts of composition. Zones where high median total price co-occurs with high median area and low median unit price are likely house-dominated zones, not necessarily more expensive per unit of housing service.
crumbs: 2  ETE TOPIC 3 :: Explanatory Spatial Econometric Models

title: 2  # ETE TOPIC 3 :: Explanatory Spatial Econometric Models
text:
6.5 A5. High-Price Support Diagnostics
High-price zones deserve scrutiny because they can disproportionately influence regression estimates and spatial autocorrelation statistics. The ranked diagnostic table combines price statistics with composition indicators to explain apparent anomalies.
What the code does: Ranks zones by unit price and total price among zones with ≥3 listings; reports listing count, IQR, median area, condition scores, property-type and preservation-class shares, fallback-assignment share, and outlier flags alongside each zone.


▶ Show code

zone_high_price_diagnostics = high_price_support_table(
    master_zones,
    id_cols=[ZONE_ID, "zone_name", "municipality_name"],
    min_n=3,
    top_n=12,
)
show_object(zone_high_price_diagnostics)

plot_layer_map(
    master_zones,
    "median_price_eur",
    "Aveiro/Ilhavo zones: median total asking price",
    cmap="YlOrRd",
)
plot_layer_map(
    master_zones,
    "median_unit_price_eur_m2",
    "Aveiro/Ilhavo zones: median unit asking price",
    cmap="YlOrRd",
)
plot_layer_map(
    master_zones,
    "median_area_living_m2",
    "Aveiro/Ilhavo zones: median dwelling area",
    cmap="PuBuGn",
)

if master_parishes is not None:
    parish_high_price_diagnostics = high_price_support_table(
        master_parishes,
        id_cols=["DTMNFR21", "freguesia"],
        min_n=3,
        top_n=12,
    )
    show_object(parish_high_price_diagnostics)
    plot_layer_map(
        master_parishes,
        "median_price_eur",
        "Aveiro/Ilhavo parishes: median total asking price",
        cmap="YlOrRd",
    )
    plot_layer_map(
        master_parishes,
        "median_unit_price_eur_m2",
        "Aveiro/Ilhavo parishes: median unit asking price",
        cmap="YlOrRd",
    )
    plot_layer_map(
        master_parishes,
        "median_area_living_m2",
        "Aveiro/Ilhavo parishes: median dwelling area",
        cmap="PuBuGn",
    )

print("High-price diagnostics are descriptive only. Neighbour comparisons will be added after spatial weights are built.")

6.5.1 Interpreting the high-price diagnostic table
Several mechanisms can produce an apparently expensive zone without reflecting genuine locational quality:

Property-type composition: a zone containing predominantly detached houses commands a higher unit price than one with mostly apartments.
Preservation-class composition: a high share of new listings inflates the zone median if new developments are systematically more expensive.
Small-N instability: a zone with 3–5 listings has a median that shifts substantially if any single listing is removed. A wide IQR for a small-N zone is a reliability warning.
Fallback assignments: a zone assigned by nearest-zone proximity may borrow the price signal of an adjacent high-price zone.
Coastal or Ria proximity: zones adjacent to the Ria de Aveiro or with water views tend to command genuine premium prices not explained by observable characteristics — these would appear as positive OLS residuals and spatially structured errors (the SEM mechanism from the lecture).

The table should not be used to drop zones silently. Flag them in the robustness matrix (section A12) instead.
crumbs: 2  ETE TOPIC 3 :: Explanatory Spatial Econometric Models

title: 2  # ETE TOPIC 3 :: Explanatory Spatial Econometric Models
text:
6.6 A6. Local Spatial Weights
The spatial weights matrix W is not a neutral technical choice. Every Moran statistic, every spatial regression coefficient, and every impact estimate is conditional on the definition of W. Different W specifications make different theoretical claims about which zones influence which and with what strength. The choice of W must be guided by theory and verified by robustness checks.
What the code does: Builds four W matrices for zones: Queen contiguity, Rook contiguity, kNN k=4, and kNN k=6. Row-standardises each (rows sum to one, so Wy is a proper weighted average of neighbours). Diagnoses each matrix: N, links, islands, components, cardinality statistics. Plots neighbour graphs for Queen and kNN-4.


▶ Show code

local_weights = {}
local_weight_diagnostics = []

zone_weight_specs = [
    ("zone_queen", lambda: build_contiguity_weights(master_zones, "queen", "Aveiro/Ilhavo zones Queen")),
    ("zone_rook", lambda: build_contiguity_weights(master_zones, "rook", "Aveiro/Ilhavo zones Rook")),
    ("zone_knn4", lambda: build_knn_weights(master_zones, 4, "Aveiro/Ilhavo zones kNN k=4")),
    ("zone_knn6", lambda: build_knn_weights(master_zones, 6, "Aveiro/Ilhavo zones kNN k=6")),
]

for key, builder in zone_weight_specs:
    name, w, diag = builder()
    local_weights[key] = w
    local_weight_diagnostics.append(diag)

display_table(pd.DataFrame(local_weight_diagnostics))

plot_neighbor_graph(master_zones, local_weights["zone_queen"], "Aveiro/Ilhavo zones: Queen neighbours")
plot_neighbor_graph(master_zones, local_weights["zone_knn4"], "Aveiro/Ilhavo zones: kNN k=4 neighbours")

parish_weights = {}
parish_weight_diagnostics = []
if master_parishes is not None and len(master_parishes) >= 5:
    parish_specs = [
        ("parish_queen", lambda: build_contiguity_weights(master_parishes, "queen", "Aveiro/Ilhavo parishes Queen")),
        (
            "parish_knn",
            lambda: build_knn_weights(
                master_parishes,
                min(4, len(master_parishes) - 1),
                f"Aveiro/Ilhavo parishes kNN k={min(4, len(master_parishes) - 1)}",
            ),
        ),
    ]
    for key, builder in parish_specs:
        name, w, diag = builder()
        parish_weights[key] = w
        parish_weight_diagnostics.append(diag)
    display_table(pd.DataFrame(parish_weight_diagnostics))
    plot_neighbor_graph(master_parishes, parish_weights["parish_queen"], "Aveiro/Ilhavo parishes: Queen neighbours")
    plot_neighbor_graph(master_parishes, parish_weights["parish_knn"], "Aveiro/Ilhavo parishes: kNN neighbours")
else:
    print("Parish/freguesia weights skipped because the layer is absent or N is too small.")

6.6.1 Interpreting the spatial weights diagnostics
Queen vs. Rook: Queen contiguity defines neighbours as zones sharing either an edge or a corner; Rook requires a shared edge. In clean polygon datasets they often agree; where they differ materially, ask whether corner-only proximity is theoretically meaningful for the housing-market process being modelled.
kNN weights: k-nearest-neighbour weights ensure every zone has exactly k neighbours, avoiding the island problem. The trade-off is that kNN imposes a fixed neighbourhood size regardless of polygon size variation and may connect zones across physical barriers — notably, the Ria de Aveiro creates a meaningful barrier that contiguity weights respect implicitly but kNN weights may not.
Islands and components: a zone with no shared boundary with any other zone is an island in the graph-theoretic sense, excluded from Moran’s I and spatial regression. One connected component is expected for a well-specified zone layer.
Why W must be diagnosed before Moran’s I: Moran’s I is the spatial autocovariance of the series weighted by W. A W matrix with many islands, disconnected components, or asymmetric cardinalities produces a Moran statistic that reflects the topology of the weights object rather than the structure of the housing market.
crumbs: 2  ETE TOPIC 3 :: Explanatory Spatial Econometric Models

title: 2  # ETE TOPIC 3 :: Explanatory Spatial Econometric Models
section: 6.7 A7. Moran’s I on the Outcome (Before OLS)
text:
6.7 A7. Moran’s I on the Outcome (Before OLS)
This Moran computation operates on the raw outcome — median log price or median log unit price — before any covariates enter the model. It answers: is the spatial distribution of zone-level prices consistent with spatial randomness under the chosen W?
What the code does: Computes Moran’s I on the selected outcome for each of the four W specifications. Reports I, expected value E[I], analytical p-value (p_norm), and permutation-based p-value (p_sim) from 999 random permutations.


▶ Show code

zone_dependent_variable = (
    "median_log_price_eur"
    if "median_log_price_eur" in master_zones.columns
    else "mean_log_price_eur"
    if "mean_log_price_eur" in master_zones.columns
    else "median_price_eur"
)

zone_outcome_series = master_zones.loc[
    master_zones["n_listings"] >= 3, zone_dependent_variable
].dropna()
zone_outcome_moran = residual_moran_table(
    zone_outcome_series,
    {
        "zone_queen": local_weights["zone_queen"],
        "zone_rook": local_weights["zone_rook"],
        "zone_knn4": local_weights["zone_knn4"],
        "zone_knn6": local_weights["zone_knn6"],
    },
    "Aveiro/Ilhavo zone outcome",
)
display_table(zone_outcome_moran)

6.7.1 Interpreting the outcome Moran’s I
Positive and significant I: values above E[I] indicate that zones with similar price levels tend to be spatially adjacent. This is consistent with all three spatial mechanisms: SAR (peer price effects), SEM (clustered unobservables), and SLX (neighbourhood covariate spillovers). The raw outcome Moran’s I does not distinguish among these mechanisms — it only confirms that a spatial pattern exists before conditioning on X.
Comparing across W specifications: if Moran’s I is significant and of similar magnitude under Queen, Rook, kNN-4, and kNN-6, the spatial pattern is robust to the definition of neighbourhood. Substantially divergent results signal W-dependence and motivate robustness checks.
Critical distinction: a significant Moran’s I on the raw outcome is not evidence of spatial spillovers in the causal sense. Moran’s I is a descriptive statistic. The decision about which spatial model to estimate depends on theory and on the residual diagnostics computed after OLS (section A9). The lecture states this explicitly: “the main criterion must be theory.”
crumbs: 2  ETE TOPIC 3 :: Explanatory Spatial Econometric Models

title: 2  # ETE TOPIC 3 :: Explanatory Spatial Econometric Models
text:
6.8 A8. Local OLS Benchmark
OLS is the starting point, not the destination. Its purposes here are: (1) establish a transparent benchmark association between zone-level prices and observable characteristics; (2) produce residuals that will be used to diagnose whether spatial structure remains after conditioning on X; and (3) provide a reference for comparing log-likelihood, AIC, and fit across spatial alternatives.
What the code does: Selects valid numeric predictors (mean log area, property-type shares, condition score, municipality dummies, log listing count) via collinearity screening. Fits HC3-robust OLS. Reports coefficient table, R², adjusted R², AIC, and BIC.


▶ Show code
# ── A8. LISA — Local Moran's I (Track A) ─────────────────────────────────────
zone_lisa = compute_lisa(zone_outcome_series, local_weights["zone_queen"])

zone_lisa_sig = zone_lisa[zone_lisa["cluster"] != "ns"].sort_values("local_i", ascending=False)
print(f"Significant LISA clusters: {len(zone_lisa_sig)} / {len(zone_lisa)} zones")
display_table(zone_lisa_sig)

plot_lisa_map(
    master_zones,
    zone_lisa,
    f"Aveiro/Ílhavo zones: LISA cluster map ({zone_dependent_variable}, Queen, p<0.05)",
)

6.8.1 Interpreting the LISA cluster map — Track A
What the map shows: Local Moran’s I decomposes the global I into zone-specific contributions. Each zone is classified into one of four quadrants of the Moran scatter plot — based on whether its own value is above or below the mean and whether its spatial lag (weighted average of neighbours) is above or below the mean — and the classification is retained only if the pseudo p-value (from 999 permutations) is below 0.05.








Colour
Quadrant
Meaning




Red
HH
High-price zone surrounded by high-price neighbours — core of an expensive cluster


Orange
HL
High-price zone surrounded by low-price neighbours — spatial outlier


Light blue
LH
Low-price zone surrounded by high-price neighbours — spatial outlier


Blue
LL
Low-price zone surrounded by low-price neighbours — core of an affordable cluster


Grey
ns
Not significant at p < 0.05



Expected pattern for Aveiro/Ílhavo: given the moderate positive global I, expect a small number of HH clusters concentrated in central, well-connected, or waterfront zones, with LL clusters in more peripheral or interior zones. HL and LH outliers identify zones whose price level is structurally at odds with their immediate neighbourhood — these warrant close inspection for data quality issues or genuine micro-market discontinuities (e.g., a zone straddling the Ria de Aveiro boundary).
Robustness note: with a limited number of zones, LISA p-values have low power. Treat borderline significant clusters (p near 0.05) as exploratory signals rather than firm conclusions. The Queen W matrix was used here; cross-check any surprising clusters against the kNN result.
Link to global I: the set of HH and LL zones corresponds to the spatial clusters that drive the positive global Moran’s I. If the global I is positive and significant but the LISA map shows very few coloured zones, the global signal is driven by a handful of influential observations rather than a widespread spatial pattern.

zone_model_table = master_zones.copy()
zone_model_table["log_n_listings"] = np.log1p(zone_model_table["n_listings"])
zone_model_table, zone_municipality_dummy_cols = add_dummy_columns(
    zone_model_table,
    "municipality_name",
    prefix="municipality",

    "mean_log_area_living_m2"

    zone_model_table,

zone_condition_predictors = (
    ["mean_condition_score"]
    if "mean_condition_score" in zone_model_table.columns
    else select_share_predictors(zone_model_table, "condition_std_share_", reference_contains="used")
)
if not zone_condition_predictors:
    zone_condition_predictors = select_share_predictors(
        zone_model_table,

zone_year_predictors = select_share_predictors(

zone_candidate_predictors = (

    + zone_property_predictors[:1]

zone_candidate_predictors = valid_numeric_predictors(zone_model_table, zone_candidate_predictors)

print(f"Zone OLS predictors: {zone_x_cols}")
show_object(tidy_statsmodels_coefficients(zone_ols_result, "Aveiro/Ilhavo zone OLS HC3"))
display_table(
    pd.DataFrame(
        [
            {
                "model": "Aveiro/Ilhavo zone OLS HC3",

6.8.2 Interpreting the OLS benchmark coefficients
The OLS model approximates a zone-level hedonic regression: log(median_price) ≈ β₀ + β₁ log(area) + β₂ property_type_share + β₃ condition_score + β₄ municipality_dummy + β₅ log(n_listings)
log(area): the price-area elasticity at zone level. A value near 1 means doubling average area approximately doubles median price (constant unit price per m²).
Municipality dummy: captures the average price difference between Aveiro and Ílhavo zones after conditioning on size and quality.
log_n_listings: guards against interpreting low-listing-count zones as low-price zones. A positive coefficient suggests denser observation zones command higher prices conditional on other characteristics.
HC3 standard errors: appropriate because zone medians computed from different numbers of listings have unequal precision. The HC3 correction uses leverage-adjusted residuals and performs well in small samples.
R² and AIC: an R² around 0.3–0.6 is typical for zone-level hedonic OLS with only compositional regressors. Spatial models will not necessarily have higher R² — the key diagnostic is whether residual spatial autocorrelation falls after adding spatial structure.

section: 6.9 A9. Residual Moran’s I and LM Diagnostics
text:
6.9 A9. Residual Moran’s I and LM Diagnostics
OLS residuals are the unexplained component of zone-level prices. If spatially random, OLS is adequate. If spatially autocorrelated, the model is missing something spatial — and the LM diagnostics help identify what.
What the code does: Computes Moran’s I on OLS residuals for all four W specifications. Runs LM diagnostics via spreg.OLS with spat_diag=True: LM-Lag, Robust LM-Lag, LM-Error, Robust LM-Error, LM-SARMA. Produces a decision table.


▶ Show code

zone_residual_moran = residual_moran_table(
    zone_ols_residuals,
    {
        "zone_queen": local_weights["zone_queen"],
        "zone_rook": local_weights["zone_rook"],
        "zone_knn4": local_weights["zone_knn4"],
        "zone_knn6": local_weights["zone_knn6"],
    },
    "Aveiro/Ilhavo zone OLS residuals",
)
display_table(zone_residual_moran)

zone_lm_diagnostics = run_spreg_ols_diagnostics(
    zone_model_df,
    zone_dependent_variable,
    zone_x_cols,
    local_weights["zone_queen"],
    "Aveiro/Ilhavo zone OLS diagnostics Queen W",
)
display_table(zone_lm_diagnostics)

zone_decision_table = build_lm_decision_table(
    zone_lm_diagnostics,
    zone_residual_moran,
    "Aveiro/Ilhavo zones",
)
display_table(zone_decision_table)

6.9.1 Interpreting the residual Moran’s I and LM tests
Residual Moran’s I: compare to the raw outcome Moran from A7. If OLS substantially reduced Moran’s I, the spatial pattern in prices was largely explainable by observed covariates. If residual Moran’s I remains large and significant, spatial structure persists in the unexplained component.
A significant residual Moran’s I does not identify the mechanism. It could arise from omitted spatially correlated covariates, endogenous price interaction (SAR), spatially autocorrelated unobservables (SEM), or spatial heterogeneity (GWR/regime models). The joint LM diagnostics are the next tool.

Elhorst (2010) decision tree — reading the joint Robust LM result:
Read Robust LM_ρ (H₀: ρ = 0) and Robust LM_λ (H₀: λ = 0) simultaneously, then enter the corresponding branch:










Robust LM_ρ
Robust LM_λ
Branch
First model to estimate
LR simplification test




Reject
Fail
Left
SDM
LR_θ (H₀: θ = 0): fail → SAR; reject → keep SDM


Fail
Fail
Middle
SLX
Test θ = 0: fail → OLS; reject → LR_ρ → SLX or SDM


Fail
Reject
Right
SDM
LR_λ Common Factor (H₀: θ + ρβ = 0): fail → SEM; reject → keep SDM


Reject
Reject
General
SDM (default)
LR_θ and LR_λ + AIC + theory



The key departure from Anselin et al. (1996): SDM is always the first spatial model when either or both robust LM tests fire. SAR and SEM are not direct selection endpoints — they are simplifications of SDM, confirmed by LR restriction tests. SLX is the natural starting model only when neither test rejects.

Applying the tree to this dataset (Aveiro/Ílhavo zones):
With a small sample (N ≈ 80–120 zones after filtering) and moderate spatial autocorrelation, the expected outcomes are:

Left branch (LM_ρ rejects, LM_λ does not): estimate SDM; run LR_θ to decide SAR vs. SDM. This branch is consistent with a price-contagion story — buyers comparing across zones drive mutual price determination.
Middle branch (neither rejects): SLX may absorb the residual structure through WX without a lag term; test θ = 0 to confirm whether neighbourhood covariates explain the remaining pattern.
Right branch (LM_λ rejects, LM_ρ does not): estimate SDM; run the Common Factor LR_λ test (H₀: θ + ρβ = 0) to decide SDM vs. SEM. This branch is consistent with spatially clustered unobservables — Ria proximity, planning constraints — that OLS cannot capture.
General case (both reject): estimate SDM; use AIC and the LR tests to determine whether ρ or λ dominates and whether the WX terms are jointly significant.

With the small zone-level sample, LM tests have moderate power. Both firing simultaneously is common when spatial structure is strong; SDM is then the principled starting point because it avoids prejudging the mechanism.
Crucial reminder: the decision tree is a formal guide, not an automatic selector. Theory governs final interpretation. If market theory predicts price contagion (buyers comparing across zones → SAR mechanism), the left branch deserves priority even if LM_λ is also marginally significant. The LR tests formalise the simplification; theory provides its substantive motivation.

title: 2  # ETE TOPIC 3 :: Explanatory Spatial Econometric Models
section: 6.10 A10. Local SLX, SAR/SLM, SEM, SDM, and SDEM
text:
6.10 A10. Local SLX, SAR/SLM, SEM, SDM, and SDEM
Each model tells a different spatial story and has different implications for interpretation and policy.
What the code does: - Estimates SLX via HC3 OLS after appending WX columns (spatially lagged covariates). - Estimates SAR/SLM and SEM via maximum likelihood using spreg.ML_Lag and spreg.ML_Error. - Estimates SDM — the Elhorst general model — by running ML_Lag on the expanded frame [X, WX_selected]. - Estimates SDEM by running ML_Error on the same expanded frame. - Computes residual Moran’s I for each model to track absorption of spatial autocorrelation. - Assembles a comparison table with fit statistics (pseudo-R², log-likelihood, AIC), spatial parameters (ρ for SAR/SDM; λ for SEM/SDEM), and residual Moran across specifications. - The LR_θ simplification (SDM → SAR) and the Common Factor LR_λ (SDM → SEM) are evaluated from the log-likelihood ratio and ΔAIC in the comparison table.


▶ Show code

zone_model_comparison_rows = []
zone_model_coefficients = []

zone_queen_resid_moran_value = None
if not zone_residual_moran.empty:
    queen_rows = zone_residual_moran[zone_residual_moran["weights"].eq("zone_queen")]
    if not queen_rows.empty:
        zone_queen_resid_moran_value = queen_rows["moran_i"].iloc[0]

zone_model_comparison_rows.append(
    ols_comparison_row(zone_ols_result, "OLS HC3", residual_moran=zone_queen_resid_moran_value)
)
zone_model_coefficients.append(tidy_statsmodels_coefficients(zone_ols_result, "OLS HC3"))

zone_slx_result, zone_slx_residuals, zone_wx_cols = fit_slx_statsmodels(
    zone_model_df,
    zone_dependent_variable,
    zone_x_cols,
    local_weights["zone_queen"],
    "Aveiro/Ilhavo zone SLX Queen W",
)
zone_slx_moran = residual_moran_table(
    zone_slx_residuals,
    {"zone_queen": local_weights["zone_queen"]},
    "Aveiro/Ilhavo zone SLX residuals",
)
zone_model_comparison_rows.append(
    ols_comparison_row(
        zone_slx_result,
        "SLX Queen",
        residual_moran=zone_slx_moran["moran_i"].iloc[0] if not zone_slx_moran.empty else None,
    )
)
zone_model_coefficients.append(tidy_statsmodels_coefficients(zone_slx_result, "SLX Queen"))

zone_sar_model, zone_sar_residuals, zone_sar_summary = fit_spreg_model(
    zone_model_df,
    zone_dependent_variable,
    zone_x_cols,
    local_weights["zone_queen"],
    "sar",
    "SAR/SLM Queen",
)
zone_sem_model, zone_sem_residuals, zone_sem_summary = fit_spreg_model(
    zone_model_df,
    zone_dependent_variable,
    zone_x_cols,
    local_weights["zone_queen"],
    "sem",
    "SEM Queen",
)

for summary, residuals, label in [
    (zone_sar_summary, zone_sar_residuals, "SAR/SLM Queen"),
    (zone_sem_summary, zone_sem_residuals, "SEM Queen"),
]:
    if len(residuals):
        moran_df = residual_moran_table(residuals, {"zone_queen": local_weights["zone_queen"]}, f"{label} residuals")
        summary["residual_moran_queen"] = moran_df["moran_i"].iloc[0] if not moran_df.empty else None
    zone_model_comparison_rows.append(summary)

zone_model_coefficients.append(tidy_spreg_coefficients(zone_sar_model, "SAR/SLM Queen"))
zone_model_coefficients.append(tidy_spreg_coefficients(zone_sem_model, "SEM Queen"))

zone_quality_variable = selected_interpretable_predictor(
    zone_x_cols,
    ["condition", "preservation", "new"],
)
zone_sdm_wx_sources = [col for col in [zone_quality_variable, zone_area_predictor] if col in zone_x_cols]
zone_sdm_model, zone_sdm_residuals, zone_sdm_summary, zone_sdm_wx_cols, zone_sdm_w = fit_sdm_model(
    zone_model_df,
    zone_dependent_variable,
    zone_x_cols,
    zone_sdm_wx_sources[:2],
    local_weights["zone_queen"],
    "SDM Queen",
)
zone_sdem_model, zone_sdem_residuals, zone_sdem_summary, zone_sdem_wx_cols, zone_sdem_w = fit_sdem_model(
    zone_model_df,
    zone_dependent_variable,
    zone_x_cols,
    zone_sdm_wx_sources[:2],
    local_weights["zone_queen"],
    "SDEM Queen",
)

for summary, residuals, label in [
    (zone_sdm_summary, zone_sdm_residuals, "SDM Queen"),
    (zone_sdem_summary, zone_sdem_residuals, "SDEM Queen"),
]:
    if len(residuals):
        moran_df = residual_moran_table(residuals, {"zone_queen": local_weights["zone_queen"]}, f"{label} residuals")
        summary["residual_moran_queen"] = moran_df["moran_i"].iloc[0] if not moran_df.empty else None
    zone_model_comparison_rows.append(summary)

zone_model_coefficients.append(tidy_spreg_coefficients(zone_sdm_model, "SDM Queen"))
zone_model_coefficients.append(tidy_spreg_coefficients(zone_sdem_model, "SDEM Queen"))

zone_model_comparison = pd.DataFrame(zone_model_comparison_rows)
zone_coefficient_table = pd.concat(
    [table for table in zone_model_coefficients if table is not None and not table.empty],
    ignore_index=True,
)
display_table(zone_model_comparison)
show_object(zone_coefficient_table)
display_table(zone_decision_table)
print(f"Zone SDM/SDEM spatially lagged X terms: {zone_sdm_wx_sources[:2]}")

Zone SDM/SDEM spatially lagged X terms: ['mean_condition_score', 'mean_log_area_living_m2']



6.10.1 Interpreting the spatial model comparison table
Reading the table in the Elhorst (2010) framework:
SDM is the general model estimated first (following the LM decision). SAR, SEM, SLX, and SDEM are either restrictions of SDM or alternative benchmarks. The comparison table provides the log-likelihoods and AIC values needed to evaluate the LR simplification tests numerically.

SLX adds WX (spatially lagged covariates) to OLS. The θ coefficient captures the average effect on zone i’s price of a one-unit change in the covariate value in zone i’s neighbours. SLX produces local spillovers only: a change in zone j affects zone i via the direct WX channel but does not propagate further. SLX is the Elhorst middle-branch model (neither Robust LM test fires). If SLX substantially absorbs residual Moran’s I compared to OLS, the spatial pattern was primarily driven by neighbourhood covariate levels, not by price contagion or correlated errors.
SAR/SLM adds Wy as an endogenous regressor. The coefficient ρ > 0 means a price shock in any zone propagates globally through the network via the spatial multiplier (I − ρW)⁻¹. Critical: β in SAR is not the marginal effect of X on Y — compute direct, indirect, and total impacts (section A11).
In the Elhorst tree, SAR emerges from SDM via the LR_θ test (H₀: θ = 0):
\[\text{LR}_\theta = 2(\ell_{\text{SDM}} - \ell_{\text{SAR}}) \sim \chi^2(q)\]
where \(q\) is the number of WX terms in SDM. If p > 0.05 (or ΔAIC ≤ 2), the restriction θ = 0 is not rejected and SAR is the preferred parsimonious model.
SEM models the error process u = λWu + ε. A positive λ means unobserved spatial factors — Ria proximity, planning constraints, neighbourhood social composition — are correlated across adjacent zones. In SEM, β retains a direct interpretation analogous to OLS. SEM is the formal LeSage–Pace limit with zero indirect effects.
SEM emerges from SDM via the Common Factor LR_λ test (H₀: θ + ρβ = 0, i.e., the Common Factor Hypothesis):
\[\text{LR}_\lambda = 2(\ell_{\text{SDM}} - \ell_{\text{SEM}}) \sim \chi^2(q)\]
If p > 0.05, SDM and SEM are statistically equivalent — the WX terms merely re-express what ρ and β already capture in the SEM error structure.
SDM is the Elhorst general model (left and right branches; default for “both reject”). It nests SAR (θ = 0) and connects to SEM via the Common Factor restriction. Retain SDM when LR_θ rejects (WX terms are jointly significant given ρ) and the Common Factor LR_λ also rejects.
SDEM combines SLX-style WX with SEM-style error correlation. Appropriate when theory predicts both neighbourhood covariate spillovers and clustered unobservables, but no endogenous price contagion. SDEM is a robustness benchmark: if SDEM and SDM yield similar AIC, the WX channel may substitute for part of what SDM assigns to ρ.
An important distinction from the lecture: in SAR, the ratio indirect/direct is the same for every predictor because all propagation runs through the single scalar ρ. In SDM, each variable has its own θ_k, allowing different spillover magnitudes per predictor. This is part of why Elhorst positions SDM as the general starting model.

Practical reading guide for the comparison table:








Comparison
Statistic
Decision rule




SDM vs. SAR (LR_θ)
2(ℓ_SDM − ℓ_SAR) ~ χ²(q)
p > 0.05 → SAR preferred


SDM vs. SEM (LR_λ, Common Factor)
2(ℓ_SDM − ℓ_SEM) ~ χ²(q)
p > 0.05 → SEM consistent with SDM


Residual Moran after SDM
I statistic
Insignificant → spatial structure absorbed


SAR vs. SEM (if both plausible)
Lower AIC + theory
Contagion (SAR) vs. unobservables (SEM)

title: 2  # ETE TOPIC 3 :: Explanatory Spatial Econometric Models
text:
6.11 A11. Local Spatial Impacts
For SAR and SDM, raw β coefficients are not marginal effects. The spatial multiplier (I − ρW)⁻¹ means a change in the covariate of any zone propagates to all other zones, feeding back to the origin zone and outward through the entire neighbour network.
What the code does: Constructs the dense N×N weight matrix; computes (I − ρW)⁻¹ via matrix inversion; for each selected variable computes the impact matrix S_k(W) = (I − ρW)⁻¹(β_k I + θ_k W); reports average diagonal (direct), average off-diagonal row sum (indirect), and their sum (total).


▶ Show code
# ── A11. Spatial Impacts ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Impacts apply only to models that contain an endogenous spatial lag (SAR, SDM).
# SEM and SDEM contain a spatial error process (λ), not a spatial lag on y,
# so their β coefficients are already direct marginal effects and their
# indirect effects are zero by construction (LeSage–Pace 2009, §2.9).

zone_y_is_log = (
    zone_dependent_variable.startswith("median_log")
    or zone_dependent_variable.startswith("mean_log")
)

# ── (1) SAR impacts — all predictors ─────────────────────────────────────────
print("=" * 70)
print("SAR/SLM — LeSage–Pace direct / indirect / total impacts (all X)")
print("=" * 70)
if zone_sar_model is not None:
    zone_sar_impacts = compute_spatial_impacts(
        zone_sar_model,
        local_weights["zone_queen"],
        zone_model_df.index,
        zone_x_cols,                   # every predictor in the zone OLS
        model_label="SAR/SLM Queen — Aveiro/Ílhavo zones",
        y_is_log=zone_y_is_log,
    )
    display_table(zone_sar_impacts)
else:
    print("SAR model not available.")
    zone_sar_impacts = pd.DataFrame()

# ── (2) SDM impacts — all predictors; theta != 0 only for WX-included vars ───
print()
print("=" * 70)
print("SDM — LeSage–Pace direct / indirect / total impacts (all X)")
print("Note: variables NOT in zone_sdm_wx_sources have theta = 0 (SAR-like)")
print("      variables IN zone_sdm_wx_sources have theta from the fitted WX term")
print("=" * 70)
if zone_sdm_model is not None:
    zone_sdm_impacts = compute_spatial_impacts(
        zone_sdm_model,
        local_weights["zone_queen"],
        zone_model_df.index,
        zone_x_cols,                   # every predictor
        wx_variables=zone_sdm_wx_sources,  # variables for which WX was estimated
        model_label="SDM Queen — Aveiro/Ílhavo zones",
        y_is_log=zone_y_is_log,
    )
    display_table(zone_sdm_impacts)
else:
    print("SDM model not available (skipped due to over-parameterisation).")
    zone_sdm_impacts = pd.DataFrame()

# ── (3) OLS β vs SAR direct impact — multiplier distortion visible ───────────
print()
print("=" * 70)
print("OLS raw β  vs  SAR direct impact (same variable, same W)")
print("The SAR direct impact exceeds |β| because the multiplier (I−ρW)⁻¹")
print("feeds back a fraction ρ of every zone's own price shock.")
print("=" * 70)
if not zone_sar_impacts.empty and "direct_impact" in zone_sar_impacts.columns:
    ols_betas = {
        row["term"]: row["estimate"]
        for _, row in tidy_statsmodels_coefficients(zone_ols_result, "OLS").iterrows()
        if row["term"] != "const"
    }
    comparison_rows = []
    for _, row in zone_sar_impacts.iterrows():
        var = row.get("variable")
        if var is None or pd.notna(row.get("note", None)):
            continue
        comparison_rows.append({
            "variable": var,
            "OLS_beta": round(float(ols_betas.get(var, float("nan"))), 4),
            "SAR_direct": round(float(row["direct_impact"]), 4),
            "SAR_indirect": round(float(row["indirect_impact"]), 4),
            "SAR_total": round(float(row["total_impact"]), 4),
            "rho": round(float(row["rho"]), 4),
            "multiplier_inflation_%": round(
                100 * (float(row["direct_impact"]) - float(ols_betas.get(var, float("nan"))))
                / abs(float(ols_betas.get(var, 1.0))), 2
            ) if var in ols_betas and ols_betas[var] != 0 else None,
        })
    if comparison_rows:
        display_table(pd.DataFrame(comparison_rows))

# ── (4) SEM — β = direct effect; indirect = 0 by construction ────────────────
print()
print("=" * 70)
print("SEM — coefficient table with explicit impact columns")
print("In SEM: direct_impact = β (same as OLS);  indirect_impact = 0")
print("The spatial process λWu is in the ERROR TERM, not in y.")
print("A change in X_i affects y_i directly via β but does NOT propagate")
print("through the neighbour network to other zones' y values.")
print("=" * 70)
if zone_sem_model is not None:
    zone_sem_coef = tidy_spreg_coefficients(zone_sem_model, "SEM Queen — Aveiro/Ílhavo zones")
    if not zone_sem_coef.empty:
        zone_sem_coef = zone_sem_coef[zone_sem_coef["term"] != "CONSTANT"].copy()
        zone_sem_coef["direct_impact"] = zone_sem_coef["estimate"]
        zone_sem_coef["indirect_impact"] = 0.0
        zone_sem_coef["total_impact"] = zone_sem_coef["estimate"]
        zone_sem_coef["impact_note"] = "SEM: β=direct; indirect=0 (LeSage–Pace framework)"
        display_table(zone_sem_coef[[
            "model", "term", "estimate", "direct_impact",
            "indirect_impact", "total_impact", "impact_note"
        ]])

# ── (5) SDEM — same zero-indirect logic as SEM ───────────────────────────────
print()
print("=" * 70)
print("SDEM — coefficient table with explicit impact columns")
print("In SDEM: β is the direct effect of own-zone X;  θ (on WX) is the")
print("         direct spillover from neighbouring zones' X values.")
print("         Indirect effects on y are zero — no endogenous spatial lag.")
print("=" * 70)
if zone_sdem_model is not None:
    zone_sdem_coef = tidy_spreg_coefficients(zone_sdem_model, "SDEM Queen — Aveiro/Ílhavo zones")
    if not zone_sdem_coef.empty:
        zone_sdem_coef = zone_sdem_coef[zone_sdem_coef["term"] != "CONSTANT"].copy()
        zone_sdem_coef["is_wx_term"] = zone_sdem_coef["term"].str.startswith("W_")
        zone_sdem_coef["direct_impact"] = zone_sdem_coef["estimate"]
        zone_sdem_coef["indirect_impact"] = 0.0
        zone_sdem_coef["total_impact"] = zone_sdem_coef["estimate"]
        zone_sdem_coef["impact_note"] = zone_sdem_coef["is_wx_term"].map({
            True: "WX term: direct spillover from neighbour X; indirect on y = 0",
            False: "Own-zone X: direct effect; indirect on y = 0",
        })
        display_table(zone_sdem_coef[[
            "model", "term", "estimate", "direct_impact",
            "indirect_impact", "total_impact", "impact_note"
        ]])

print(f"\nZone dependent variable: {zone_dependent_variable}")
print(f"Zone SAR ρ = {zone_sar_impacts['rho'].iloc[0]:.4f}" if not zone_sar_impacts.empty and "rho" in zone_sar_impacts.columns and len(zone_sar_impacts) else "")
print(f"Zone SDM ρ = {zone_sdm_impacts['rho'].iloc[0]:.4f}" if not zone_sdm_impacts.empty and "rho" in zone_sdm_impacts.columns and len(zone_sdm_impacts) else "")
print(f"SDM WX variables: {zone_sdm_wx_sources}")

6.11.1 Interpreting the spatial impact decomposition — Track A

Which models produce impacts, and why

Core rule: indirect effects (spillovers on y) only exist in models that contain an endogenous spatial lag ρWy. SEM and SDEM have a spatial process in the error term, not in y itself; their β coefficients are direct effects, and their indirect effects on y are zero.




Reading the SAR/SLM impact table
direct_impact is the average diagonal of S_k(W) = (I−ρW)⁻¹ β_k I. Because the multiplier (I−ρW)⁻¹ has positive diagonal elements greater than 1 (for ρ > 0), the direct impact is larger in absolute value than the raw OLS β. The difference is the feedback loop: a rise in zone i’s predictor raises i’s price, which raises neighbours’ prices slightly, which feeds back to raise i’s price again.
indirect_impact is the average row sum of the off-diagonal elements of S_k(W). For SAR, the ratio indirect/direct is the same for every predictor because all propagation runs through the single scalar ρ. A large ρ produces large indirect effects relative to direct effects.
OLS β vs SAR direct (comparison table above): the “multiplier_inflation_%” column shows how much larger the SAR direct impact is relative to the raw OLS coefficient. Reading OLS β as a marginal effect when ρ is non-negligible will underestimate the true own-zone effect.



Reading the SDM impact table
SDM adds θ_k for each WX variable in the sdm_wx_sources list. - Variables with a WX term (θ_k ≠ 0): direct impact = (I−ρW)⁻¹(β_k + θ_k·row_of_W); indirect impact reflects both ρ propagation and the direct WX channel θ_k. - Variables without a WX term (θ_k = 0): the SDM impact formula reduces to the SAR formula; indirect/direct ratio equals ρ.
This variable-specific spillover flexibility is the key empirical advantage of SDM over SAR.



Reading the SEM and SDEM tables
In SEM, every row of the displayed table shows: - estimate = β, the coefficient from ML_Error - direct_impact = β (identical to OLS β after SEM correction for clustered errors) - indirect_impact = 0.0 — no propagation of X changes through the y network
The spatial process λWu absorbs the clustered unobservables (Ria proximity, neighbourhood social capital, planning constraints) but does not create a mechanism by which a change in zone j’s observable X raises zone i’s price.
In SDEM, the WX terms (θ) capture first-order, local spillovers from neighbouring zones’ observable covariates — but again these enter y directly (not via a multiplier). A change in zone j’s condition score raises zone i’s price by θ × w_ij (the weight for that pair), and the effect stops there: it does not propagate further to zone i’s other neighbours.



Practical guidance for scale interpretation
If the dependent variable is log price, all impacts are approximately proportional: - A direct impact of 0.05 ≈ 5 % higher own-zone price per one-unit increase in the predictor. - An indirect impact of 0.03 ≈ 3 % higher prices in neighbouring zones on average.
For share predictors (e.g., condition shares ranging 0–1): - Multiply impacts by 0.10 to get the effect of a 10-percentage-point change.
For log predictors (e.g., log population density): - Multiply impacts by ln(1.1) ≈ 0.095 to get the effect of a 10 % increase in density.

zone_robustness_y = existing_columns(
    master_zones,
    ["median_log_price_eur", "median_log_unit_price_eur_m2", "median_price_eur"],
)
zone_robustness_matrix = run_ols_robustness_matrix(
    zone_model_table,
    zone_robustness_y,
    zone_x_cols,
    {
        "zone_queen": local_weights["zone_queen"],
        "zone_rook": local_weights["zone_rook"],
        "zone_knn4": local_weights["zone_knn4"],
        "zone_knn6": local_weights["zone_knn6"],
    },
    min_n_values=[None, 3, 5],
    outlier_col="unit_price_outlier_share",
    fallback_col="fallback_assignment_share",
    label="Aveiro/Ilhavo zones",
)
display_table(zone_robustness_matrix)

if master_parishes is not None and parish_weights:
    parish_y = existing_columns(master_parishes, ["median_log_price_eur", "median_log_unit_price_eur_m2", "median_price_eur"])
    parish_x_candidates = valid_numeric_predictors(
        master_parishes,
        existing_columns(master_parishes, [zone_area_predictor, "mean_condition_score", "n_listings"]),
    )
    if parish_y and parish_x_candidates:
        parish_robustness_matrix = run_ols_robustness_matrix(
            master_parishes,
            parish_y[:2],
            parish_x_candidates[:3],
            parish_weights,
            min_n_values=[None, 3, 5],
            outlier_col="unit_price_outlier_share",
            fallback_col="fallback_assignment_share",
            label="Aveiro/Ilhavo parishes",
        )
        display_table(parish_robustness_matrix)
    else:
        print("Parish robustness skipped because dependent or predictor variables are insufficient.")

6.11.2 Interpreting the robustness matrix
Stable residual Moran across W specs: if all four W matrices produce a significant positive residual Moran after OLS, the spatial structure in prices is not specific to any one neighbourhood definition. This strengthens the case for a spatial model.
Sensitivity to minimum-listing-count thresholds: if results change substantially when zones with <3 or <5 listings are excluded, small-N instability is influencing the aggregate pattern.
Sensitivity to outlier and fallback filters: large changes in R² or residual Moran when high-outlier-share or high-fallback-share zones are removed indicate those zones disproportionately drive the spatial pattern. Investigate those zones before drawing substantive conclusions.

title: 2  # ETE TOPIC 3 :: Explanatory Spatial Econometric Models
text:
7.1 B1. Load Municipal Data
What the code does: Loads CAOP24_CONT_MUNI and the optional boundary-only cont_municipios fallback. Repairs invalid geometries and reprojects to EPSG:3763. Creates log-transformed price targets. Reports a load summary.


▶ Show code

muni = read_gpkg_layer(GPKG_PATH, detected_layers["municipal"], "mainland municipal analytical layer")
muni_boundaries = (
    read_gpkg_layer(
        GPKG_PATH,
        detected_layers["municipal_boundary_fallback"],
        "mainland municipal boundary fallback",
    )
    if detected_layers.get("municipal_boundary_fallback")
    else None
)

muni, muni_geometry_repair = repair_invalid_geometries(muni, "municipal analytical layer")
muni_boundaries, muni_boundaries_geometry_repair = repair_invalid_geometries(
    muni_boundaries, "municipal boundary fallback"
)

muni_3763 = to_metric_crs(muni, "municipal analytical layer", PROJECTED_CRS)
muni_boundaries_3763 = (
    to_metric_crs(muni_boundaries, "municipal boundary fallback", PROJECTED_CRS)
    if muni_boundaries is not None
    else None
)

municipal_load_summary = pd.DataFrame(
    [
        {
            "role": "municipal analytical layer",
            "layer": detected_layers["municipal"],
            "rows": len(muni_3763),
            "geometry_type": ", ".join(sorted(muni_3763.geom_type.dropna().unique())),
            "source_crs": str(muni.crs),
            "analysis_crs": str(muni_3763.crs),
        },
        {
            "role": "boundary fallback",
            "layer": detected_layers.get("municipal_boundary_fallback"),
            "rows": len(muni_boundaries_3763) if muni_boundaries_3763 is not None else 0,
            "geometry_type": ", ".join(sorted(muni_boundaries_3763.geom_type.dropna().unique()))
            if muni_boundaries_3763 is not None
            else None,
            "source_crs": str(muni_boundaries.crs) if muni_boundaries is not None else None,
            "analysis_crs": str(muni_boundaries_3763.crs) if muni_boundaries_3763 is not None else None,
        },
    ]
)

display_table(municipal_load_summary)
display_table(pd.DataFrame([muni_geometry_repair, muni_boundaries_geometry_repair]))

7.1.1 Interpreting the municipal load summary
The municipal analytical layer (CAOP24_CONT_MUNI) is a pre-assembled modelling table combining CAOP 2024 polygon geometries with housing-price targets and 18 socio-economic and geographic indicators for all 278 mainland Portugal municipalities. Row count should be exactly 278; CRS after reprojection should be EPSG:3763 (PT-TM06).
Log-price targets: log_sales_median_eur_m2_2024_total, log_sales_median_eur_m2_2024_existing, log_rent_median_eur_m2_2023, and log_sales_median_eur_m2_2024_new are created by add_log_if_positive. Log transformation compresses the extreme right tail (Lisbon and Algarve coastal municipalities exceed €4,000/m²) and makes log-point interpretation directly applicable to regression coefficients.
Primary regression outcome: log_sales_median_eur_m2_2024_total has coverage for 276 of the 278 municipalities. Only two extreme rural interior municipalities (Barrancos, Alentejo and Penedono, Norte) lack sufficient transaction data for a median estimate. This 99.3% coverage means all downstream models operate on a near-complete national cross-section rather than a filtered sub-sample, making the spatial statistics (Moran’s I, ρ, λ) directly interpretable as national-level parameters.
crumbs: 2  ETE TOPIC 3 :: Explanatory Spatial Econometric Models

title: 2  # ETE TOPIC 3 :: Explanatory Spatial Econometric Models
text:
7.2 B2. Municipal Quality Assurance
What the code does: Identifies the municipal identifier column (dtmn); catalogues available price-target and indicator columns; reports missingness for each variable; checks for duplicate municipality IDs; creates log-transformed copies of all positively-valued price targets; warns about beds_per_resident (completely empty — 0/278 non-null values, not usable) and share_tourism_beds_al (271/278 non-null — minor missingness in rural interior).


▶ Show code

municipal_id_candidates = existing_columns(muni_3763, ["dtmn", "geo", "municipio"])
municipal_id_col = municipal_id_candidates[0] if municipal_id_candidates else None

housing_price_vars = existing_columns(muni_3763, MUNICIPAL_PRICE_TARGETS)
socio_economic_indicators = existing_columns(muni_3763, MUNICIPAL_SOCIO_ECONOMIC_INDICATORS)
geographic_indicators = existing_columns(muni_3763, MUNICIPAL_GEOGRAPHIC_INDICATORS)

municipal_variable_roles = pd.DataFrame(
    [
        {"role": "housing_price_target", "variable": var} for var in housing_price_vars
    ]
    + [{"role": "socio_economic_indicator", "variable": var} for var in socio_economic_indicators]
    + [{"role": "geographic_indicator", "variable": var} for var in geographic_indicators]
)
display_table(municipal_variable_roles)

display_table(missingness_summary(muni_3763, housing_price_vars))
display_table(missingness_summary(muni_3763, socio_economic_indicators + geographic_indicators))

if municipal_id_col:
    display_table(duplicate_summary(muni_3763, municipal_id_col))
else:
    print("No municipal identifier candidate found among dtmn, geo, municipio.")

display_table(positive_value_summary(muni_3763, housing_price_vars))

municipal_modelling_table = muni_3763.copy()
for var in housing_price_vars:
    municipal_modelling_table = add_log_if_positive(municipal_modelling_table, var)

for caution_col in ["beds_per_resident", "share_tourism_beds_al"]:
    if caution_col in municipal_modelling_table.columns:
        non_null = int(municipal_modelling_table[caution_col].notna().sum())
        unique_non_null = municipal_modelling_table[caution_col].dropna().nunique()
        print(f"{caution_col}: non-null={non_null}, unique non-null values={unique_non_null}")

municipal_target_samples = []
for var in housing_price_vars:
    sample = municipal_modelling_table.loc[municipal_modelling_table[var].notna()].copy()
    municipal_target_samples.append(
        {
            "target": var,
            "n_non_missing_municipalities": len(sample),
            "log_variable": f"log_{var}" if f"log_{var}" in municipal_modelling_table.columns else None,
        }
    )
display_table(pd.DataFrame(municipal_target_samples))

7.2.1 Interpreting the municipal QA step
Target coverage after data correction:









Target
Non-missing
Coverage
Nature of missingness




sales_median_eur_m2_2024_total
276/278
99.3 %
2 extreme rural interior municipalities (Barrancos, Penedono) — trivially small distortion


sales_median_eur_m2_2024_existing
274/278

4 rural interior municipalities; excludes new-build premium


rent_median_eur_m2_2023

sales_median_eur_m2_2024_new

Sparse; concentrated in urban/suburban areas with active new-build pipelines



The sales coverage (276/278) is near-complete. All regression and Moran analyses in B6–B9 use log_sales_median_eur_m2_2024_total as the primary outcome unless otherwise specified.
Structural missingness in the rental target: the 75 municipalities missing rent_median_eur_m2_2023 are not randomly absent — they are low-population rural areas (Alentejo interior, Trás-os-Montes, Beira Interior) with no sustained formal rental market. This is structural market absence, not a data collection failure. If rent is used as the outcome, the spatial weight matrix must be subsetted to the 203 observed municipalities, altering the connectivity structure of the estimation. Do not impute missing rents.
beds_per_resident is completely empty (0/278): this column is not usable and will be silently excluded by valid_numeric_predictors. Note this in any sensitivity analysis involving tourism indicators.
Full indicator coverage: all 10 socio-economic indicators (population_density through recent_to_old_building_ratio) and 10 geographic indicators (share_artificialized through tourism_beds_density) are fully populated across all 278 municipalities, sourced from the 2021 Census, administrative registers, and INE. No imputation is required for the predictor side.

title: 2  # ETE TOPIC 3 :: Explanatory Spatial Econometric Models
section: 7.3 B3. Municipal Indicators and Correlation Structure
text:
7.3 B3. Municipal Indicators and Correlation Structure
The 18 indicators span six theoretical families: urban intensity and demographic pressure, human capital and labour market, housing stock composition, land use, accessibility and services, and tourism intensity. The correlation matrix — now estimated on the full national cross-section of 278 municipalities — reveals which families empirically overlap.
What the code does: Displays descriptive statistics for price targets and indicator groups. Computes the inter-indicator correlation matrix. Prints the recommended parsimonious predictor set for first-pass OLS, balancing multicollinearity control with theoretical coverage.


▶ Show code

display_table(numeric_summary(municipal_modelling_table, housing_price_vars))
display_table(numeric_summary(municipal_modelling_table, socio_economic_indicators))
display_table(numeric_summary(municipal_modelling_table, geographic_indicators))

candidate_numeric_indicators = existing_columns(
    municipal_modelling_table,
    [
        "population_density",
        "share_education_higher",
        "employment_rate",
        "secondary_residence_share",
        "vacant_total_share",
        "share_artificialized",
        "share_green_land",
        "poi_density_total",
        "essential_poi_density",
        "leisure_tourism_poi_density",
        "tourism_beds_density",
    ],
)

if candidate_numeric_indicators:
    indicator_correlation = (
        municipal_modelling_table[candidate_numeric_indicators]
        .apply(pd.to_numeric, errors="coerce")
        .corr()
        .round(2)
    )
    show_object(indicator_correlation)

recommended_municipal_predictors = existing_columns(
    municipal_modelling_table,
    [
        "population_density",
        "share_education_higher",
        "share_age_65plus",
        "secondary_residence_share",
        "share_artificialized",
        "tourism_beds_density",
        "poi_density_total",
    ],
)
print("Recommended small first-pass municipal predictor set:")
print(recommended_municipal_predictors)

Recommended small first-pass municipal predictor set:
['population_density', 'share_education_higher', 'share_age_65plus', 'secondary_residence_share', 'share_artificialized', 'tourism_beds_density', 'poi_density_total']



7.3.1 Interpreting the indicator correlation structure
With indicators fully populated across all 278 municipalities, the correlation matrix captures the true national cross-sectional association structure.
Urban collinearity cluster: population_density, share_artificialized, poi_density_total, and share_education_higher are all high in urban municipalities simultaneously — expect |r| > 0.7 within this cluster. Including all in a single OLS would produce severe multicollinearity and inflated standard errors. The recommended predictor set selects one representative per theoretical family.
The secondary-residence duality: the raw correlation between secondary_residence_share and sales price is actually slightly negative (approximately r ≈ −0.38 nationally). This counterintuitive result reflects a structural duality in the data: secondary residence share is high in two fundamentally different contexts — (1) Algarve coastal municipalities (very high prices, driven by international tourism investment and second-home demand) and (2) rural interior municipalities (very low prices, secondary homes owned by emigrants or retirees). These two groups pull the correlation in opposite directions. The OLS coefficient for secondary_residence_share will depend on which group dominates in the estimation sample and whether other covariates (especially tourism_beds_density and log_population_density) absorb the between-group heterogeneity.
Two distinct high-price mechanisms: the national data contains two structurally different pathways to high prices: 1. Metropolitan human-capital mechanism (Lisboa, Porto, Oeiras, Cascais): high density, high education share (27–36 %), high POI density, moderate secondary residence (~6–12 %). 2. Coastal tourism mechanism (Algarve municipalities — Lagos, Loulé, Aljezur, Vila do Bispo, Albufeira): low density (19–314 persons/km²), low education share (~13–15 %), very high secondary residence (39–47 %), very high tourism beds. Education alone predicts this group poorly; secondary residence and tourism density are the key identifiers.
A single linear OLS does not cleanly separate these two mechanisms. Spatial models — particularly SEM and SDEM, which absorb spatially clustered unobservables such as the Algarve sub-market premium — can partly correct for this heterogeneity in the error structure.
The recommended parsimonious predictor set selects one representative from each family, conscious of the duality above: - Urban intensity: log_population_density - Human capital: share_education_higher (strongest bivariate predictor, r ≈ 0.71) - Age structure: share_age_65plus - Housing stock / tourist demand: secondary_residence_share (enters with ambiguous sign; the context is resolved partly by including log_tourism_beds_density) - Land use: share_artificialized - Tourism / accessibility: log_tourism_beds_density (r ≈ 0.45; captures Algarve and Lisboa demand concentration)

title: 2  # ETE TOPIC 3 :: Explanatory Spatial Econometric Models
text:
7.4 B4. Municipal Exploratory Mapping
Choropleth maps at municipal resolution provide the national-scale spatial context for all models that follow.
What the code does: Produces choropleth maps of the main price targets, missingness maps for each price variable, and choropleth maps of all main socio-economic and geographic indicators.


▶ Show code

municipal_high_price_diagnostics = high_price_municipal_table(
    municipal_modelling_table,
    min_nonmissing=True,
    top_n=15,
)
show_object(municipal_high_price_diagnostics)

municipal_missingness_overview = missingness_summary(
    municipal_modelling_table,
    housing_price_vars
    + existing_columns(
        municipal_modelling_table,
        [
            "population_density",
            "share_education_higher",
            "employment_rate",
            "secondary_residence_share",
            "vacant_total_share",
            "share_artificialized",
            "share_green_land",
            "poi_density_total",
            "tourism_beds_density",
        ],
    ),
)
display_table(municipal_missingness_overview)

for var in existing_columns(
    municipal_modelling_table,
    ["sales_median_eur_m2_2024_total", "sales_median_eur_m2_2024_existing", "rent_median_eur_m2_2023"],
):
    plot_layer_map(municipal_modelling_table, var, f"Mainland Portugal municipalities: {var}")
    missing_flag = f"{var}_is_missing"
    municipal_modelling_table[missing_flag] = municipal_modelling_table[var].isna()
    plot_layer_map(
        municipal_modelling_table,
        missing_flag,
        f"Mainland Portugal municipalities: missingness for {var}",
        cmap="Set2",
    )

for var in existing_columns(
    municipal_modelling_table,
    [
        "population_density",
        "share_education_higher",
        "employment_rate",
        "secondary_residence_share",
        "vacant_total_share",
        "tourism_beds_density",
        "share_artificialized",
        "share_green_land",
        "poi_density_total",
        "essential_poi_density",
        "leisure_tourism_poi_density",
    ],
):
    plot_layer_map(municipal_modelling_table, var, f"Mainland Portugal municipalities: {var}", cmap="plasma")

7.4.1 Interpreting the municipal maps
Price choropleth maps reveal two spatially distinct high-price clusters that confirm the structural duality identified in B3:

Metropolitan corridor: Lisboa (€4,340/m²), Cascais (€4,053), Oeiras (€3,471), and Porto (€2,984) form dense, contiguous high-price clusters in the Great Lisbon and Porto Metropolitan Areas. The spatial gradient is steep: moving one or two municipalities inland from the core, prices drop by 30–50 %. This gradient motivates the SAR mechanism — price contagion spreading outward from the metropolitan core through demand-displacement cascades.
Algarve coast: Lagos (€3,452), Loulé (€3,333), Aljezur (€3,059), Vila do Bispo (€3,030), Albufeira (€2,941), Tavira (€2,853) form a second contiguous cluster. These municipalities have fundamentally different predictor profiles: low density (19–314 persons/km²), low education (13–15 %), but secondary residence shares of 39–47 % and high tourism bed density. This cluster is driven by international tourism investment and second-home demand, not local labour-market dynamics.

Interior municipalities (Alentejo, Beira Interior, Trás-os-Montes) form large contiguous low-price zones (€208–€500/m²). This creates a strong global spatial gradient that generates very high positive Moran’s I.
Missingness maps for sales: only 2 municipalities are missing — Barrancos (Alentejo, pop. ~1,700) and Penedono (Norte, pop. ~2,600). The estimation sample is functionally the entire mainland Portugal. These two absences create no material distortion in spatial weight matrices, Moran statistics, or regression coefficients.
Missingness maps for rent: 75 municipalities missing, geographically concentrated in interior regions. The missingness map will show a clearly non-random spatial pattern — a broad band of missing observations across the interior. This confirms structural market absence rather than random data loss. If the rent target is used in a sensitivity analysis (B10), the spatial weight matrix must be subsetted accordingly.
Indicator maps and their relationship to the price map: - population_density: high in Lisboa, Porto, Braga corridor — visually matches the metropolitan price cluster. - share_education_higher: mirrors population density but with lower spatial contrast — useful as a human-capital demand indicator. - secondary_residence_share: shows high values in TWO distinct spatial zones — Algarve coast and parts of Trás-os-Montes / Beira Interior — reflecting the duality discussed in B3. - tourism_beds_density: highly concentrated in Lisboa, Porto, and Algarve — visually matches the price map better than secondary residence for identifying high-price locations. - share_artificialized: follows the population density map, confirming high urban-rural correlation.
The visual co-variation between these indicator maps and the price map motivates the regression but does not establish causality. Unobserved regional factors (the SEM mechanism) — Algarve as a structurally distinct sub-market, coastal proximity premium, NUTS2-level governance — are plausible confounders at this scale.

text:
7.5 B5. Municipal Spatial Weights
What the code does: Builds four W matrices on the full 278-municipality polygon layer (Queen, Rook, kNN-4, kNN-6), row-standardises each, diagnoses each for islands and cardinality, and plots neighbour graphs for Queen and kNN-4 specifications.


▶ Show code

municipal_weights = {}
municipal_weight_diagnostics = []

municipal_weight_specs = [
    ("municipal_queen", lambda: build_contiguity_weights(municipal_modelling_table, "queen", "Mainland municipalities Queen")),
    ("municipal_rook", lambda: build_contiguity_weights(municipal_modelling_table, "rook", "Mainland municipalities Rook")),
    ("municipal_knn4", lambda: build_knn_weights(municipal_modelling_table, 4, "Mainland municipalities kNN k=4")),
    ("municipal_knn6", lambda: build_knn_weights(municipal_modelling_table, 6, "Mainland municipalities kNN k=6")),
]

for key, builder in municipal_weight_specs:
    name, w, diag = builder()
    municipal_weights[key] = w
    municipal_weight_diagnostics.append(diag)

display_table(pd.DataFrame(municipal_weight_diagnostics))

plot_neighbor_graph(
    municipal_modelling_table,
    municipal_weights["municipal_queen"],
    "Mainland Portugal municipalities: Queen neighbours",
)
plot_neighbor_graph(
    municipal_modelling_table,
    municipal_weights["municipal_knn4"],
    "Mainland Portugal municipalities: kNN k=4 neighbours",
)

7.5.1 Interpreting the municipal weights diagnostics
All four weight matrices are built on the complete 278-municipality polygon layer. With 276/278 municipalities available for the primary regression sample, the spatial weight matrix used in estimation covers essentially the full national adjacency structure. This is qualitatively different from a subsampled W: subsampled matrices lose links at the boundary of the observed sample, potentially attenuating Moran statistics and spatial model parameters.
Size heterogeneity: Portugal’s municipal boundaries span an order-of-magnitude range in area. Alentejo municipalities (Mértola: 1,292 km²; Castro Verde: 568 km²) have typically 3–5 contiguous neighbours. Minho and Douro municipalities (Braga: 183 km², some as small as 8 km²) may have 8–12 neighbours. Queen contiguity preserves this natural heterogeneity, which is structurally informative — large rural municipalities with few neighbours form genuine spatial isolation in the price formation process. kNN-4 and kNN-6 impose a uniform neighbourhood size that helps statistical power in sparse regions but may artificially link geographically distant municipalities.
Islands (expected: zero): mainland Portugal’s municipal boundary topology is well-defined with no maritime municipalities entirely surrounded by other municipalities. Verify n_islands = 0 for Queen and Rook. Any non-zero island count indicates a geometry problem in the source GPKG (sliver polygons, topology gaps).
Queen vs. Rook similarity: for mainland Portugal, Queen and Rook contiguity produce nearly identical neighbour lists because most boundaries share extended edges rather than single corner points. Small cardinality differences at irregular boundary junctions (typically ±1 neighbour per municipality) should produce essentially the same Moran statistics and model conclusions.
Robustness criterion: the four W matrices serve as the robustness grid in B10. Spatial dependence findings that are consistent across Queen, Rook, kNN-4, and kNN-6 are robust to the adjacency definition and represent genuine spatial structure rather than a W-artefact.
