text:
7.6 B6. Municipal Moran’s I on Outcomes and Indicators
What the code does: Computes Moran’s I on the selected housing-price outcome across all four W specifications (using the subsample of 276 non-missing municipalities). Computes indicator Moran’s I under Queen W. Displays results in tabular form.


▶ Show code

municipal_target_preference = [
    "log_sales_median_eur_m2_2024_total",
    "log_sales_median_eur_m2_2024_existing",
    "log_rent_median_eur_m2_2023",
    "sales_median_eur_m2_2024_total",
    "sales_median_eur_m2_2024_existing",
    "rent_median_eur_m2_2023",
]
municipal_dependent_variable = next(
    col for col in municipal_target_preference if col in municipal_modelling_table.columns
)

municipal_outcome_series = municipal_modelling_table[municipal_dependent_variable].dropna()
municipal_outcome_moran = residual_moran_table(
    municipal_outcome_series,
    {
        "municipal_queen": municipal_weights["municipal_queen"],
        "municipal_rook": municipal_weights["municipal_rook"],
        "municipal_knn4": municipal_weights["municipal_knn4"],
        "municipal_knn6": municipal_weights["municipal_knn6"],
    },
    "Municipal housing-price outcome",
)
display_table(municipal_outcome_moran)

municipal_indicator_moran_targets = existing_columns(
    municipal_modelling_table,
    [
        "population_density",
        "share_education_higher",
        "secondary_residence_share",
        "tourism_beds_density",
        "share_artificialized",
        "poi_density_total",
    ],
)
indicator_moran_rows = []
for indicator in municipal_indicator_moran_targets:
    indicator_series = municipal_modelling_table[indicator].dropna()
    indicator_moran = residual_moran_table(
        indicator_series,
        {"municipal_queen": municipal_weights["municipal_queen"]},
        f"Municipal indicator: {indicator}",
    )
    indicator_moran["variable"] = indicator
    indicator_moran_rows.append(indicator_moran)
if indicator_moran_rows:
    display_table(pd.concat(indicator_moran_rows, ignore_index=True))

7.6.1 Interpreting the municipal Moran tables
Price outcome Moran’s I (276 municipalities, near-complete national sample): with 99.3 % coverage, this statistic is very close to the true population-level spatial autocorrelation of Portuguese municipal housing prices. Expect a large positive and highly significant Moran I — national price data with the strong coastal-metropolitan vs. interior gradient typically produce I > 0.5 under Queen weights. The statistical significance (p < 0.001) will be highly robust given 276 observations.
Compare Moran I values across the four W specifications: - Queen and Rook should produce nearly identical I values (≈ ±0.01 difference) given their similarity for mainland Portugal boundaries. - kNN-4 and kNN-6 may produce slightly lower I because they connect some geographically distant municipalities (e.g., large Alentejo municipalities whose centroids are close despite not sharing a boundary) that do not form genuine price-market neighbourhoods. - Consistent statistical significance across all four W specifications is the key robustness check: it confirms that spatial clustering of prices is a structural feature of the national market, not an artefact of the adjacency definition.
Indicator Moran’s I — interpreting spatially structured predictors: - population_density: expected I > 0.5 (dense municipalities are contiguous in Lisboa, Porto, Braga/Guimarães metropolitan clusters). - share_education_higher: expected I > 0.5 (mirrors population density; university cities cluster regionally). - secondary_residence_share: expected I 0.3–0.5. Its spatial pattern is more fragmented — both Algarve and rural interior municipalities have high values but these two groups are separated by low-value interior-urban municipalities. - tourism_beds_density: expected I > 0.5, driven by extreme concentration in Lisboa/Porto urban areas and Algarve. - share_artificialized: expected I > 0.6, strongly urban-gradient.
Why high indicator Moran I motivates spatial modelling: even if OLS perfectly captures the indicator-price relationship in the non-spatial sense, the spatial structure within the indicators means that OLS residuals will absorb any spatial structure in prices not fully explained by the observed X variables — and that unexplained spatial structure generates autocorrelated residuals. The LM tests (B7) identify whether residual autocorrelation is SAR-type (contagion in y) or SEM-type (clustering in unobservables).
Rent vs. sales Moran comparison (if available): the rent Moran I is estimated on 203 municipalities, deliberately excluding the lowest-price rural interior. The rental sample therefore has a smaller spatial price gradient than the sales sample. Expect rent Moran I to be lower than sales Moran I — not because rental markets are less spatially integrated, but because the systematic exclusion of the low-price extremes compresses the spatial variance that drives Moran statistics.

text:
7.7 B7. Municipal OLS Benchmark
What the code does: Log-transforms population_density, tourism_beds_density, and poi_density_total (right-skewed, spanning 2–3 orders of magnitude). Prepares the model frame: drops municipalities with any missing value on the outcome or any predictor, producing a clean cross-section of approximately 270–276 observations (depending on how many predictors have any missing value — all indicators are 278/278, so losses are driven by the price target only). Fits HC3-robust OLS; stores residuals. Maps OLS residuals onto all 278 municipal polygons (missing-target municipalities shown in grey). Computes residual Moran’s I and LM diagnostics under all four W specifications. Prints the LM decision table.


▶ Show code
# ── B8. LISA — Local Moran's I (Track B) ─────────────────────────────────────
municipal_lisa = compute_lisa(municipal_outcome_series, municipal_weights["municipal_queen"])

municipal_lisa_sig = municipal_lisa[municipal_lisa["cluster"] != "ns"].sort_values("local_i", ascending=False)
print(f"Significant LISA clusters: {len(municipal_lisa_sig)} / {len(municipal_lisa)} municipalities")
display_table(municipal_lisa_sig)

plot_lisa_map(
    muni_3763,
    municipal_lisa,
    f"Mainland Portugal: LISA cluster map ({municipal_dependent_variable}, Queen, p<0.05)",
)

7.7.1 Interpreting the LISA cluster map — Track B
What the map shows: the same Local Moran’s I logic as Track A, now applied at the municipal scale across all 278 mainland Portugal municipalities. With near-complete national coverage the LISA map amounts to a spatial atlas of housing-price clustering.








Colour
Quadrant
Expected geography




Red
HH
Lisboa Metropolitan Area (Oeiras, Cascais, Sintra, Loures), Porto Metropolitan Area (Porto, Matosinhos, Maia), and Algarve coastal municipalities (Lagos, Albufeira, Loulé, Faro)


Orange
HL
Isolated high-price municipalities in a lower-price region — may include Coimbra, Évora, or small Algarve interior municipalities adjacent to lower-price neighbours


Light blue
LH
Low-price municipalities on the fringes of metropolitan areas — interior municipalities adjacent to the Lisboa or Porto belt


Blue
LL
Interior centre-north and Alentejo municipalities (Guarda, Beja, Portalegre districts)


Grey
ns
Not significant at p < 0.05



Reading the coast–interior gradient: the dominant pattern in Portuguese municipal housing data is a strong coastal-metropolitan vs. interior gradient. The LISA map should show a contiguous HH belt along the Atlantic coast and Algarve, and a contiguous LL belt across the interior. The HL and LH outliers identify municipalities where local conditions override the regional gradient.
Comparison with global I: with 276 non-missing observations and expected I > 0.5, virtually all municipalities in clearly clustered regions (Greater Lisboa, Greater Porto, Algarve coast, interior Alentejo) should appear as significant HH or LL. A large share of grey municipalities would indicate that price clustering is weaker than the global statistic suggests once local variance is taken into account.
Modelling implications: HH and LL municipalities are candidate locations for spatially lagged effects (SAR/SLM) or clustered unobservables (SEM). HL and LH outliers are candidates for spatial regimes or regime-switching extensions if the standard spatial models leave large residuals for those units.


▶ Show code

municipal_model_table = municipal_modelling_table.copy()
if "population_density" in municipal_model_table.columns:
    municipal_model_table = add_log_if_positive(
        municipal_model_table,
        "population_density",
        "log_population_density",
    )
if "tourism_beds_density" in municipal_model_table.columns:
    municipal_model_table = add_log_if_positive(
        municipal_model_table,
        "tourism_beds_density",
        "log_tourism_beds_density",

if "poi_density_total" in municipal_model_table.columns:
    municipal_model_table = add_log_if_positive(
        municipal_model_table,
        "poi_density_total",

    municipal_model_table,

        "log_population_density",

    municipal_model_table,
    municipal_dependent_variable,

municipal_ols_result, municipal_ols_residuals = fit_hc3_ols(
    municipal_model_df,

municipal_model_table["ols_residual_log_price"] = np.nan
municipal_model_table.loc[municipal_ols_residuals.index, "ols_residual_log_price"] = municipal_ols_residuals
municipal_modelling_table["ols_residual_log_price"] = municipal_model_table["ols_residual_log_price"]

print(f"Municipal dependent variable: {municipal_dependent_variable}")

    pd.DataFrame(

            {
                "model": "Municipal OLS HC3",

    )
)

plot_layer_map(
    municipal_modelling_table,

    cmap="coolwarm",
)

municipal_residual_moran = residual_moran_table(
    municipal_ols_residuals,
    {
        "municipal_queen": municipal_weights["municipal_queen"],
        "municipal_rook": municipal_weights["municipal_rook"],
        "municipal_knn4": municipal_weights["municipal_knn4"],
        "municipal_knn6": municipal_weights["municipal_knn6"],
    },
    "Municipal OLS residuals",
)
display_table(municipal_residual_moran)

municipal_lm_diagnostics = run_spreg_ols_diagnostics(
    municipal_model_df,
    municipal_dependent_variable,
    municipal_x_cols,
    municipal_weights["municipal_queen"],
    "Municipal OLS diagnostics Queen W",
)
display_table(municipal_lm_diagnostics)

municipal_decision_table = build_lm_decision_table(
    municipal_lm_diagnostics,
    municipal_residual_moran,
    "Mainland municipalities",
)
display_table(municipal_decision_table)

7.7.2 Interpreting the municipal OLS benchmark
Expected coefficient signs with the full 276-municipality national sample:

log_population_density: positive (likely the most robust predictor). Dense municipalities concentrate employment, amenities, and market liquidity. Consistent across both high-price mechanisms (Lisboa/Porto and Algarve urban centres like Faro and Portimão).
share_education_higher: positive, with the strongest bivariate correlation (r ≈ 0.71). Lisboa, Porto, Oeiras, and Cascais combine the highest education shares with the highest prices. However, the Algarve high-price municipalities have low education shares (13–15 %), so the coefficient partially understates the role of education in metropolitan areas while being attenuated by the tourism-driven mechanism.
share_age_65plus: negative. Municipalities with older demographic structures tend to be rural interior, economically less dynamic, and experiencing depopulation — all of which depress housing demand and prices.
secondary_residence_share: sign ambiguous in OLS (bivariate correlation r ≈ −0.38 nationally). The duality identified in B3 — high secondary residence in both high-price Algarve AND low-price rural interior — means the OLS coefficient depends critically on which other covariates are in the model. Without log_tourism_beds_density or log_population_density to differentiate the two groups, this coefficient may be near zero or negative. With those controls, it may turn positive for the Algarve-identified segment. Interpret with caution.
share_artificialized: positive (more built-up land correlates with urban intensity and land scarcity), though largely absorbed by log_population_density given their high correlation.
log_tourism_beds_density: positive (r ≈ 0.45). Captures the Algarve and Lisboa high-price areas through the tourism demand channel. This predictor may help resolve the secondary_residence_share ambiguity: once tourism density is controlled for, secondary residence may better isolate the rural emigrant-home effect.

Model fit expectations: OLS R² should be in the range 0.60–0.80 with the recommended 5–6 predictors on the national sample. The selected indicators explain a large share of the log-price variation because the dominant drivers (density, education, tourism) are well captured. The remaining unexplained variation is largely spatial — visible in the residual map.
Residual map interpretation: after conditioning on the selected indicators, expect two classes of systematic residual pattern: 1. Positive residuals (under-predicted prices) in municipalities near the Lisboa and Porto metropolitan cores — adjacent municipalities benefit from agglomeration spillovers (commuter access, labour market integration) not fully captured by their own indicators. 2. Positive residuals in Algarve interior municipalities where the tourism-coastal premium partially fails to be explained by measured tourism density or secondary residence due to the duality problem described above. 3. Near-zero residuals across much of the interior, where low prices are well explained by the indicator values. Any large spatially contiguous cluster of same-sign residuals directly motivates SAR or SEM specifications — these clusters signal either contagion (SAR mechanism) or unobserved regional factors (SEM mechanism).
Elhorst (2010) LM decision — municipal scale: with 270+ observations and a strong national price gradient, both Robust LM_ρ and Robust LM_λ are likely to reject simultaneously. Apply the joint reading:

At the national scale, the dual-mechanism structure — metropolitan demand-displacement contagion (SAR-type) and Algarve regional unobservable (SEM-type) — makes simultaneous rejection of both Robust LM tests the most likely outcome. SDM is then the principled first model in B8. Simplification toward SAR (LR_θ) or SEM (Common Factor LR_λ) is tested formally, not assumed.

title: 2  # ETE TOPIC 3 :: Explanatory Spatial Econometric Models
text:
7.8 B8. Municipal Spatial Models
What the code does: Estimates five spatial models (SLX, SAR/SLM, SEM, SDM, SDEM) using the Queen W matrix on the ~270–276 municipality estimation sample. Assembles the model comparison table (log-likelihood, AIC, residual Moran after each model). Identifies the most interpretable predictor (municipal_priority_variable) for WX construction in SDM and SDEM. Displays full coefficient tables for all models.


▶ Show code

municipal_model_comparison_rows = []
municipal_model_coefficients = []

municipal_queen_resid_moran_value = None
if not municipal_residual_moran.empty:
    queen_rows = municipal_residual_moran[municipal_residual_moran["weights"].eq("municipal_queen")]
    if not queen_rows.empty:
        municipal_queen_resid_moran_value = queen_rows["moran_i"].iloc[0]

municipal_model_comparison_rows.append(
    ols_comparison_row(municipal_ols_result, "OLS HC3", residual_moran=municipal_queen_resid_moran_value)
)
municipal_model_coefficients.append(tidy_statsmodels_coefficients(municipal_ols_result, "OLS HC3"))

municipal_slx_result, municipal_slx_residuals, municipal_wx_cols = fit_slx_statsmodels(
    municipal_model_df,
    municipal_dependent_variable,
    municipal_x_cols,
    municipal_weights["municipal_queen"],
    "Municipal SLX Queen W",
)
municipal_slx_moran = residual_moran_table(
    municipal_slx_residuals,
    {"municipal_queen": municipal_weights["municipal_queen"]},
    "Municipal SLX residuals",
)
municipal_model_comparison_rows.append(
    ols_comparison_row(
        municipal_slx_result,
        "SLX Queen",
        residual_moran=municipal_slx_moran["moran_i"].iloc[0] if not municipal_slx_moran.empty else None,
    )
)
municipal_model_coefficients.append(tidy_statsmodels_coefficients(municipal_slx_result, "SLX Queen"))

municipal_sar_model, municipal_sar_residuals, municipal_sar_summary = fit_spreg_model(
    municipal_model_df,
    municipal_dependent_variable,
    municipal_x_cols,
    municipal_weights["municipal_queen"],
    "sar",
    "SAR/SLM Queen",
)
municipal_sem_model, municipal_sem_residuals, municipal_sem_summary = fit_spreg_model(
    municipal_model_df,
    municipal_dependent_variable,
    municipal_x_cols,
    municipal_weights["municipal_queen"],
    "sem",
    "SEM Queen",
)

for summary, residuals, label in [
    (municipal_sar_summary, municipal_sar_residuals, "SAR/SLM Queen"),
    (municipal_sem_summary, municipal_sem_residuals, "SEM Queen"),
]:
    if len(residuals):
        moran_df = residual_moran_table(
            residuals,
            {"municipal_queen": municipal_weights["municipal_queen"]},
            f"{label} residuals",
        )
        summary["residual_moran_queen"] = moran_df["moran_i"].iloc[0] if not moran_df.empty else None
    municipal_model_comparison_rows.append(summary)

municipal_model_coefficients.append(tidy_spreg_coefficients(municipal_sar_model, "SAR/SLM Queen"))
municipal_model_coefficients.append(tidy_spreg_coefficients(municipal_sem_model, "SEM Queen"))

municipal_priority_variable = selected_interpretable_predictor(
    municipal_x_cols,
    ["population_density", "education", "poi", "tourism", "artificialized", "secondary"],
)
municipal_sdm_wx_sources = [col for col in [municipal_priority_variable] if col in municipal_x_cols]
municipal_sdm_model, municipal_sdm_residuals, municipal_sdm_summary, municipal_sdm_wx_cols, municipal_sdm_w = fit_sdm_model(
    municipal_model_df,
    municipal_dependent_variable,
    municipal_x_cols,
    municipal_sdm_wx_sources,
    municipal_weights["municipal_queen"],
    "SDM Queen",
)
municipal_sdem_model, municipal_sdem_residuals, municipal_sdem_summary, municipal_sdem_wx_cols, municipal_sdem_w = fit_sdem_model(
    municipal_model_df,
    municipal_dependent_variable,
    municipal_x_cols,
    municipal_sdm_wx_sources,
    municipal_weights["municipal_queen"],
    "SDEM Queen",
)

for summary, residuals, label in [
    (municipal_sdm_summary, municipal_sdm_residuals, "SDM Queen"),
    (municipal_sdem_summary, municipal_sdem_residuals, "SDEM Queen"),
]:
    if len(residuals):
        moran_df = residual_moran_table(
            residuals,
            {"municipal_queen": municipal_weights["municipal_queen"]},
            f"{label} residuals",
        )
        summary["residual_moran_queen"] = moran_df["moran_i"].iloc[0] if not moran_df.empty else None
    municipal_model_comparison_rows.append(summary)

municipal_model_coefficients.append(tidy_spreg_coefficients(municipal_sdm_model, "SDM Queen"))
municipal_model_coefficients.append(tidy_spreg_coefficients(municipal_sdem_model, "SDEM Queen"))

municipal_model_comparison = pd.DataFrame(municipal_model_comparison_rows)
municipal_coefficient_table = pd.concat(
    [table for table in municipal_model_coefficients if table is not None and not table.empty],
    ignore_index=True,
)
display_table(municipal_model_comparison)
show_object(municipal_coefficient_table)
display_table(municipal_decision_table)
print(f"Municipal SDM/SDEM spatially lagged X terms: {municipal_sdm_wx_sources}")

7.8.1 Interpreting the municipal spatial model comparison
Reading the table in the Elhorst (2010) framework:
SDM is the general model in B8, estimated first when either or both Robust LM tests fire. The comparison table provides log-likelihoods and AIC values for the LR simplification tests that determine whether SAR or SEM is a justified parsimonious representation.

SLX at the municipal scale: WX coefficients describe whether neighbouring municipalities’ predictor levels influence a focal municipality’s prices beyond what that municipality’s own indicators explain. A positive θ on W_log_population_density signals commuter integration and labour-market access — a genuine spatial externality. A positive θ on W_log_tourism_beds_density suggests a tourism agglomeration zone effect: municipalities adjacent to intense tourism destinations benefit from tourist overspill and investment proximity. SLX does not model contagion in the outcome (no ρ) — it models local spatial spillovers in the predictors only. SLX is the Elhorst middle-branch model, relevant if — contrary to national-scale expectations — neither Robust LM test fires.
SAR at the municipal scale: a positive and significant ρ confirms that municipal housing prices are mutually determined. Given the strong national price gradient (€208 to €4,340/m²), ρ is likely substantial (ρ ≈ 0.4–0.7). Through the multiplier (I − ρW)⁻¹, a price surge in Lisboa propagates to adjacent Loures, Sintra, and Setúbal — demand-displacement — which then propagates further to second-ring municipalities. The size of ρ determines cascade extent.
In the Elhorst tree, SAR is reached from SDM via the LR_θ test (H₀: θ = 0): \[\text{LR}_\theta = 2(\ell_{\text{SDM}} - \ell_{\text{SAR}}) \sim \chi^2(q)\] If p > 0.05 (or ΔAIC ≤ 2), the WX terms are not earning their parameterisation and SAR is the more parsimonious model. Given the strong national gradient and heterogeneous spillover magnitudes, θ ≠ 0 is plausible, and SDM may well be retained.
SEM at the municipal scale: a positive λ means spatially clustered unobservables dominate the error structure: (1) the Algarve sub-market premium (international capital, foreign buyer demand, coastal lifestyle not fully captured by any indicator); (2) metropolitan agglomeration economies beyond measured density and education. SEM is reached from SDM via the Common Factor LR_λ test (H₀: θ + ρβ = 0): \[\text{LR}_\lambda = 2(\ell_{\text{SDM}} - \ell_{\text{SEM}}) \sim \chi^2(q)\] If p > 0.05, SDM and SEM are statistically equivalent — the WX terms merely re-express what ρ and β already capture. If p < 0.05, the WX channel is real and SDM is preferred.
SDM is the Elhorst general starting model (left branch, right branch, and the “both reject” general case that is most likely at national scale). It nests SAR and links to SEM via the Common Factor restriction. SDM’s variable-specific θ_k is theoretically attractive: tourism density, education share, and population density plausibly have different spillover magnitudes, which SAR’s single ρ cannot capture.
SDEM is a robustness benchmark. If SDEM and SDM yield similar AIC, the WX channel may partially substitute for ρ in absorbing spatial autocorrelation — worth noting but not a basis for preferring SDEM without a theoretical motivation for absent price contagion.

LR simplification guide for B8:








Comparison
Statistic
Decision




SDM vs. SAR (LR_θ)
2(ℓ_SDM − ℓ_SAR) ~ χ²(q)
p > 0.05 → SAR preferred


SDM vs. SEM (Common Factor LR_λ)
2(ℓ_SDM − ℓ_SEM) ~ χ²(q)
p > 0.05 → SEM consistent with SDM


Residual Moran after SDM
I statistic
Insignificant → spatial structure absorbed


SAR vs. SEM (if both plausible)
ΔAIC + theory
Contagion (SAR) vs. regional unobservables (SEM)




Residual Moran progression: track how residual Moran decreases from OLS → SLX → SAR → SDM. A large drop from OLS to SAR confirms the endogenous lag absorbs substantial autocorrelation. A further drop from SAR to SDM confirms WX terms carry additional spatial information. Persistent significance after SDM may reflect the Algarve sub-market unobservable or non-linearity not captured by the log-linear specification.
crumbs: 2  ETE TOPIC 3 :: Explanatory Spatial Econometric Models

title: 2  # ETE TOPIC 3 :: Explanatory Spatial Econometric Models
text:
7.9 B9. Municipal Spatial Impacts
What the code does: Computes direct, indirect, and total LeSage–Pace impacts for SAR (all predictors) and SDM (all predictors, with θ applied only where WX was estimated). Builds the OLS β vs. SAR direct comparison table with multiplier inflation. Displays SEM and SDEM coefficient tables with explicit direct = β, indirect = 0 annotations. Prints ρ estimates for both SAR and SDM.


▶ Show code
# ── B9. Spatial Impacts ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Impacts apply only to models with an endogenous spatial lag (SAR, SDM).
# SEM and SDEM: β = direct effect; indirect effect on y = 0 by construction.

muni_y_is_log = municipal_dependent_variable.startswith("log_")

# ── (1) SAR impacts — all predictors ─────────────────────────────────────────
print("=" * 70)
print("SAR/SLM — LeSage–Pace direct / indirect / total impacts (all X)")
print("=" * 70)
if municipal_sar_model is not None:
    municipal_sar_impacts = compute_spatial_impacts(
        municipal_sar_model,
        municipal_weights["municipal_queen"],
        municipal_model_df.index,
        municipal_x_cols,              # every predictor in the municipal OLS
        model_label="SAR/SLM Queen — Mainland municipalities",
        y_is_log=muni_y_is_log,
    )
    display_table(municipal_sar_impacts)
else:
    print("SAR model not available.")
    municipal_sar_impacts = pd.DataFrame()

# ── (2) SDM impacts — all predictors ─────────────────────────────────────────
print()
print("=" * 70)
print("SDM — LeSage–Pace direct / indirect / total impacts (all X)")
print("Note: variables NOT in municipal_sdm_wx_sources have theta = 0 (SAR-like)")
print("      variables IN municipal_sdm_wx_sources have theta from the fitted WX term")
print("=" * 70)
if municipal_sdm_model is not None:
    municipal_sdm_impacts = compute_spatial_impacts(
        municipal_sdm_model,
        municipal_weights["municipal_queen"],
        municipal_model_df.index,
        municipal_x_cols,              # every predictor
        wx_variables=municipal_sdm_wx_sources,
        model_label="SDM Queen — Mainland municipalities",
        y_is_log=muni_y_is_log,
    )
    display_table(municipal_sdm_impacts)
else:
    print("SDM model not available (skipped due to over-parameterisation).")
    municipal_sdm_impacts = pd.DataFrame()

# ── (3) OLS β vs SAR direct impact ───────────────────────────────────────────
print()
print("=" * 70)
print("OLS raw β  vs  SAR direct impact — multiplier distortion")
print("=" * 70)
if not municipal_sar_impacts.empty and "direct_impact" in municipal_sar_impacts.columns:
    muni_ols_betas = {
        row["term"]: row["estimate"]
        for _, row in tidy_statsmodels_coefficients(municipal_ols_result, "OLS").iterrows()
        if row["term"] != "const"
    }
    muni_comparison_rows = []
    for _, row in municipal_sar_impacts.iterrows():
        var = row.get("variable")
        if var is None or pd.notna(row.get("note", None)):
            continue
        muni_comparison_rows.append({
            "variable": var,
            "OLS_beta": round(float(muni_ols_betas.get(var, float("nan"))), 4),
            "SAR_direct": round(float(row["direct_impact"]), 4),
            "SAR_indirect": round(float(row["indirect_impact"]), 4),
            "SAR_total": round(float(row["total_impact"]), 4),
            "rho": round(float(row["rho"]), 4),
        })
    if muni_comparison_rows:
        display_table(pd.DataFrame(muni_comparison_rows))

# ── (4) SEM — β = direct effect; indirect = 0 ────────────────────────────────
print()
print("=" * 70)
print("SEM — coefficient table with explicit impact columns")
print("In SEM: direct_impact = β;  indirect_impact = 0 by construction")
print("=" * 70)
if municipal_sem_model is not None:
    muni_sem_coef = tidy_spreg_coefficients(
        municipal_sem_model, "SEM Queen — Mainland municipalities"
    )
    if not muni_sem_coef.empty:
        muni_sem_coef = muni_sem_coef[muni_sem_coef["term"] != "CONSTANT"].copy()
        muni_sem_coef["direct_impact"] = muni_sem_coef["estimate"]
        muni_sem_coef["indirect_impact"] = 0.0
        muni_sem_coef["total_impact"] = muni_sem_coef["estimate"]
        muni_sem_coef["impact_note"] = "SEM: β=direct; indirect=0 (LeSage–Pace)"
        display_table(muni_sem_coef[[
            "model", "term", "estimate", "direct_impact",
            "indirect_impact", "total_impact", "impact_note"
        ]])

# ── (5) SDEM — β = direct; WX = local spillover; indirect on y = 0 ────────────
print()
print("=" * 70)
print("SDEM — coefficient table with explicit impact columns")
print("In SDEM: β (own-X) and θ (WX) are both direct effects;")
print("         no endogenous lag ⟹ indirect effects on y = 0")
print("=" * 70)
if municipal_sdem_model is not None:
    muni_sdem_coef = tidy_spreg_coefficients(
        municipal_sdem_model, "SDEM Queen — Mainland municipalities"
    )
    if not muni_sdem_coef.empty:
        muni_sdem_coef = muni_sdem_coef[muni_sdem_coef["term"] != "CONSTANT"].copy()
        muni_sdem_coef["is_wx_term"] = muni_sdem_coef["term"].str.startswith("W_")
        muni_sdem_coef["direct_impact"] = muni_sdem_coef["estimate"]
        muni_sdem_coef["indirect_impact"] = 0.0
        muni_sdem_coef["total_impact"] = muni_sdem_coef["estimate"]
        muni_sdem_coef["impact_note"] = muni_sdem_coef["is_wx_term"].map({
            True: "WX: local spillover from neighbour X; indirect on y = 0",
            False: "Own-zone X: direct effect only",
        })
        display_table(muni_sdem_coef[[
            "model", "term", "estimate", "direct_impact",
            "indirect_impact", "total_impact", "impact_note"
        ]])

print(f"\nMunicipal dependent variable: {municipal_dependent_variable}")
print(f"Municipal SAR ρ = {municipal_sar_impacts['rho'].iloc[0]:.4f}" if not municipal_sar_impacts.empty and "rho" in municipal_sar_impacts.columns and len(municipal_sar_impacts) else "")
print(f"Municipal SDM ρ = {municipal_sdm_impacts['rho'].iloc[0]:.4f}" if not municipal_sdm_impacts.empty and "rho" in municipal_sdm_impacts.columns and len(municipal_sdm_impacts) else "")

7.9.1 Interpreting the spatial impact decomposition — Track B

Which models produce impacts, and why

Core rule: indirect effects on y exist only when the model contains an endogenous spatial lag ρWy. SEM and SDEM absorb spatial structure in the error term, not in y; no multiplier propagates shocks through the outcome network.




Reading the SAR impacts at municipal scale
With the near-complete national sample (276 municipalities) and a substantial ρ (expected 0.4–0.7), the SAR multiplier creates economically meaningful gaps between OLS β and the LeSage–Pace decomposition:

OLS_beta vs. SAR_direct: the SAR direct impact exceeds the OLS coefficient in absolute value. The multiplier amplifies the own-municipality effect because a shock to municipality i raises i’s price (β), which through ρ raises i’s neighbours’ prices, which feeds back into i’s price again. At ρ = 0.6, this feedback loop adds roughly 10–15 % to the effective direct effect for most predictors.
SAR_indirect: the average off-diagonal effect — how much a one-unit increase in municipality i’s predictor raises the average price of all other 275 municipalities through the ρ chain. With 276 municipalities in the system, the aggregate indirect effect (= N × SAR_indirect) can be several times the direct effect for highly significant ρ.
SAR_total = direct + indirect: the full national aggregate of the effect of a localised shock. For log-price outcomes, multiply by 0.10 to interpret a 10-pp predictor change in approximate percentage terms.
multiplier_inflation_%: (SAR_direct − OLS_beta) / |OLS_beta| × 100. Shows how much OLS understates the true own-municipality effect. Expect values of 5–20 % for most predictors at typical ρ values.

Reading log-scale impacts: all impacts are in log-point terms when the outcome is log_sales_median_eur_m2_2024_total. A SAR_direct of 0.14 for share_education_higher means a 10-pp increase in higher-education share is associated with exp(0.14 × 0.10) − 1 ≈ 1.4 % higher own-municipality sales price (direct effect) plus the indirect effect on all other municipalities.



Reading the SDM impacts at municipal scale
SDM allows each predictor its own θ_k spillover coefficient. Compare the SDM and SAR indirect columns for the priority variable (municipal_priority_variable):

SDM_indirect > SAR_indirect: θ > 0 — the direct WX channel amplifies spillover beyond what ρ alone produces. For log_tourism_beds_density, this is plausible if tourism agglomeration creates zone effects: being surrounded by tourist-intensive municipalities raises prices through supply-side (investment) and demand-side (tourist overspill) channels, independently of ρ.
SDM_indirect ≈ SAR_indirect: the WX term does not add material spillover; SAR is a sufficient approximation for this variable.
SDM_indirect < SAR_indirect (θ < 0): the WX term partially offsets ρ propagation. For secondary_residence_share, a negative θ is plausible because municipalities surrounded by high secondary-residence rural areas are themselves typically in the low-price rural interior — the neighbourhood context adds a price-depressing signal that partially counteracts the ρ-driven propagation.
Common Factor Hypothesis (exercise B.3): if θ̂ ≈ −ρ̂ β̂ for the priority variable, SDM simplifies to SEM. Formal testing of this restriction guides the choice between the contagion and error-clustering interpretations.




SEM and SDEM: explicit zero indirect effects on y
Why SEM indirect = 0: in y = Xβ + u, u = λWu + ε, a change in X_i affects y_i via β. It does not enter any other municipality’s structural equation. The λWu process captures spatially clustered unobservables — the Algarve sub-market premium, metropolitan agglomeration rents, coastal geography — that are autocorrelated in the error term. Correcting for this clustering changes β’s efficiency and standard errors but does not create propagation through the y network. SEM β coefficients are therefore interpretable as direct marginal effects, corrected for the bias induced by omitted spatially clustered variables.
Why SDEM indirect = 0 on y: in y = Xβ + WXθ + u, u = λWu + ε, WXθ enters y as an observable regressor — a first-order local spillover from neighbours’ X values. A change in municipality j’s predictor raises municipality i’s price by θ × w_ij (a local effect proportional to the spatial weight). This does not propagate further through a multiplier chain. SDEM therefore has a richer spillover structure than SEM (first-order neighbour X effects are made explicit) but still no global contagion.
Which model is most defensible for Portugal? Both theoretical mechanisms have strong support: - SAR mechanism: demand-displacement cascades from Lisboa and Porto to adjacent municipalities are empirically documented in Portuguese housing literature. ρ > 0 is almost certain. - SEM mechanism: the Algarve sub-market is structurally distinct — driven by international capital, not local income — creating a spatially clustered unobservable that measured indicators cannot fully capture. λ > 0 is also expected. - SDM tests whether WX adds over SAR; SDEM tests whether WX adds over SEM. The AIC comparison and residual Moran progression (in B8 and B10) provide empirical guidance. Substantive theory suggests that both ρ (contagion) and λ (unobserved regional heterogeneity) are genuinely present in the Portuguese national housing market.



Policy interpretation of the total impact at national scale
With 276 municipalities in the system, the aggregate national impact of a localised policy intervention is substantial. Consider a housing-policy scenario targeting share_education_higher:

A 10-pp increase in higher-education share in one municipality (e.g., a university expansion in Évora, Alentejo):

Direct effect: ≈ SAR_direct × 0.10 in log-points on Évora’s own median sales price.
Indirect effect: distributed across all 275 other municipalities through the ρ chain — Évora’s price increase stimulates adjacent municipality prices, which stimulate their neighbours, etc.
Total national effect: SAR_total × 0.10 — the sum of the direct effect and all indirect effects across the entire national housing system.

For secondary_residence_share, the policy interpretation is more complex due to the structural duality: a 10-pp increase in secondary residence in a coastal municipality (Algarve context) has a different mechanism than the same change in a rural interior municipality. The average SAR impact reflects both contexts and may understate the coastal tourism effect while overstating the rural effect.

This is the central policy implication of spatial econometrics: ignoring spatial interdependence (the OLS baseline) underestimates the full national impact of localised housing-market interventions by the ratio SAR_total / OLS_beta.

7.9.2 Interpreting the municipal robustness matrix
The robustness matrix systematically tests whether the spatial dependence finding and model conclusions survive changes in: (1) W specification (Queen, Rook, kNN-4, kNN-6); (2) the price outcome (sales total, sales existing, rent); and (3) whether the extreme top 1 % of price values is excluded (primarily Lisboa, Cascais, Oeiras — the extreme outliers of the national distribution).
Interpreting the results:

Residual Moran consistent across W specs: if all four W specifications produce statistically significant positive residual Moran after OLS, the spatial structure finding is robust to the adjacency definition. This is the expected result given the strong national price gradient.
Elhorst LM entry consistent across W specs: if the joint Robust LM_ρ / LM_λ pattern (i.e., which branch is entered) is the same across Queen, Rook, kNN-4, and kNN-6, the spatial dependence is a structural feature of the national housing market rather than an artefact of the adjacency definition. The LR simplification conclusions — SAR vs. SDM (LR_θ) and SEM vs. SDM (Common Factor LR_λ) — should also be stable across W specs to be credible.
Sales total vs. sales existing: both have near-complete coverage (276 and 274 municipalities). Model conclusions should be highly consistent. Minor coefficient differences are expected because the existing-sales market excludes new developments (concentrated in urban peripheries), which carry a structural price premium from newer building standards and amenities.
Rent as outcome (203 municipalities): if ρ (or λ) and coefficient signs are consistent with the sales model, the spatial mechanism generalises across tenure types. If they diverge — e.g., rent Moran lower, rent ρ smaller — this suggests that the rental market has different spatial integration, consistent with rent regulation, sticky rental prices, or sorting of lower-income households into rental housing predominantly in urban cores rather than spreading across the national system.
Excluding top 1 % of prices: removing the 2–3 most extreme municipalities tests whether the strong spatial dependence finding is driven by the Lisboa/Cascais/Oeiras outliers rather than the structural national pattern. If ρ drops substantially after exclusion, the spatial dependence is partly a leverage effect of extreme observations. If ρ remains large, the finding is genuinely distributed across the national housing system.


Comparing Track A and Track B conclusions:








Dimension
Track A (zones, Aveiro/Ílhavo)
Track B (municipalities, mainland Portugal)




N
~131 zones, ~80–120 after filtering
~276 municipalities for sales (near-complete national)


Scale
Sub-municipal, listing-based aggregation
National, administrative polygon boundaries


Price range
Moderate heterogeneity within Aveiro/Ílhavo metropolitan area
Extreme heterogeneity: €208–€4,340/m²


High-price mechanism
Zone characteristics, listing composition, zone amenity mix
Two structural mechanisms: metropolitan human capital and coastal tourism demand


Key W challenge
Physical barrier (Ria de Aveiro severs natural adjacency)
Size heterogeneity (Alentejo: large rural polygons vs. Minho: small dense polygons)


Key data challenge
MAUP, small-N zones, coordinate quality, listing composition bias
Structural missingness in rent target; secondary residence duality


Expected spatial parameter
Moderate ρ/λ (zone-level price contagion)
Larger ρ/λ (national gradient + metropolitan cascade + Algarve unobservable)


Interpretation
Zone median as sub-municipal housing-market segment
Municipal median as national administrative aggregate



If both tracks show positive, significant residual Moran after OLS, the spatial structure of housing prices operates at both local and national scales simultaneously. This multi-scale spatial dependence is theoretically expected: housing-market integration is a hierarchical process — within a city, zone-to-zone contagion follows neighbourhood comparison; across the country, municipal price diffusion follows demand displacement from metropolitan cores and regional demand shocks from national policy, tourism, and labour-market dynamics.
The two mechanisms differ: Track A spatial dependence reflects primarily the local transmission of price information and amenity diffusion within a geographically bounded urban system. Track B spatial dependence reflects the large-scale demand-displacement cascade from metropolitan cores, the structural duality between human-capital and tourism-driven markets, and spatially clustered unobservables that no single administrative dataset can fully capture.

title: 2  # ETE TOPIC 3 :: Explanatory Spatial Econometric Models
text:
9.1 Notebook structure
Two sequential tracks sharing a common setup. Both run from the project root with data/ETE_Lab.gpkg available. All helper functions are packaged in lab3_package/; notebook cells contain the narrative, configuration, and result interpretation.

title: 2  # ETE TOPIC 3 :: Explanatory Spatial Econometric Models
text:
9.2 Package structure
lab3_package/
├── __init__.py       # re-exports all public functions and constants
├── config.py         # shared constants (CRS codes, column names, indicator lists)
├── utils.py          # display_table, show_object, package_status
├── paths.py          # find_project_root, find_file
├── io.py             # GeoPackage I/O, CRS helpers, analysis-stack lazy importers
├── prep.py           # data cleaning, feature engineering, zone aggregation
├── explore.py        # choropleth maps, point maps, ranked diagnostic tables
├── weights.py        # spatial weights construction and diagnostics
└── models.py         # OLS, SLX, SAR/SLM, SEM, SDM, SDEM, Moran, LM, impacts

title: 2  # ETE TOPIC 3 :: Explanatory Spatial Econometric Models
text:
9.4 Recommended run order

Run Setup cells: package check, path configuration, helper imports.
Run GeoPackage inspection (Part 0): confirm all layers are detected.
Run Track A data loading and QA (A1–A5): confirm listings and zones load correctly.
Run Track A spatial weights (A6): diagnose W before Moran computations.
Run Track A OLS, diagnostics, and core spatial models (A7–A10).
Run Track A impacts and robustness (A11–A12): read robustness matrix before conclusions.
Repeat the same order for Track B (B1–B10).
crumbs: 2  ETE TOPIC 3 :: Explanatory Spatial Econometric Models

title: 2  # ETE TOPIC 3 :: Explanatory Spatial Econometric Models
text:
9.5 Guardrails

Do not compute distances or kNN weights in EPSG:4326 (geographic coordinates).
Do not drop outlier zones, sparse supports, or high-fallback-share zones silently.
Do not interpret SAR or SDM β coefficients as marginal effects — always compute impacts.
Do not treat LM tests as automatic model selectors — use theory as the primary criterion.
Do not use causal language unless a causal identification strategy is explicitly described.
Do not mix Track A and Track B results without accounting for the fundamental differences in support, variable definitions, and spatial processes.
